algo_problems.title: Contains Duplicate
thinking_questions: If you compared every pair, how many comparisons for n elements?
What data structure checks membership in O(1) average time?
What are you trading when you use a HashSet instead of nested loops?

hints.L1: Think about what you need to track as you scan — which data structure lets you check 'seen this before?' in O(1)?
hints.L2: Use a HashSet. For each element, check if it already exists — if yes, return true. If no, add it. One pass.
hints.L3:
// INSIGHT: trade O(n) space for O(1) lookup → O(n) instead of O(n²)
seen = set()
for num in nums:
    if num in seen:   # O(1) avg
        return True
    seen.add(num)
return False
# Alt: sort → check adjacent pairs → O(n log n) time, O(1) space

follow_up_questions: What if you needed to return the duplicate value itself?
With O(1) space required, what's your approach and complexity tradeoff?
When would you prefer sort+scan over HashSet?
algo_problems.solution_notes: HashSet one-pass: O(n) time, O(n) space. Sort alt: O(n log n), O(1) space.

algo_problems.title: Valid Anagram
thinking_questions: What property must every character in s satisfy for t to be an anagram?
If you counted character frequencies in both strings, what would anagrams have in common?
The alphabet has 26 lowercase letters — does space complexity actually depend on n?

hints.L1: Two strings are anagrams if they use identical characters with identical frequencies — think about how to verify that efficiently.
hints.L2: Frequency counter: increment for each char in s, decrement for each char in t. If all counts are zero at end, they're anagrams.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: anagram ⟺ identical char frequency maps
if len(s) != len(t): return False
count = [0] * 26
for i in range(len(s)):
    count[ord(s[i]) - ord('a')] += 1
    count[ord(t[i]) - ord('a')] -= 1
return all(c == 0 for c in count)
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(1) — fixed 26-char alphabet
# Unicode follow-up: use dict instead of fixed array

follow_up_questions: What if input could contain Unicode? How does your solution change?
Can you solve it O(n) time AND O(1) space simultaneously?
How would you check if t is a permutation of any substring of s?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Frequency counter array[26]: O(n) time, O(1) space. Length check first as early exit.

algo_problems.title: Two Sum
thinking_questions: For each number x, you need target-x. How do you find it without scanning the whole array again?
What if you built a lookup table as you went — what would you store?
Why does this problem need indices, not just values?

hints.L1: For each number, check if its complement (target minus that number) has already appeared — store what you've seen so far.
hints.L2: HashMap {value: index}. For each num, check if (target-num) already in map. If yes, return both indices. If no, add current.

follow_up_questions: Why must we check before inserting, not after? Walk through [3,3] target=6.
If the array were sorted, how would you solve it differently?
What if multiple valid pairs existed — return all of them?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Check-then-insert one-pass HashMap. O(n) time, O(n) space. Key: [3,3] target=6 edge case.

algo_problems.title: Group Anagrams
thinking_questions: What property do all anagrams of a word share that you could use as a dictionary key?
If you sorted each word, what would all anagrams sort to?
Is there a canonical 'fingerprint' for a word that doesn't require sorting?

hints.L1: You need a key that all anagrams of a word share — think about what operation produces identical results for anagrams.
hints.L2: Sort each word to get its canonical key. All anagrams sort identically. Group words by sorted key in a HashMap.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: sort word → canonical key; all anagrams share the same key
from collections import defaultdict
groups = defaultdict(list)
for word in strs:
    key = ''.join(sorted(word))  # 'eat','tea','ate' all → 'aet'
    groups[key].append(word)
return list(groups.values())
# Time: O(n·k log k)  Space: O(n·k)
# Faster key: tuple of 26 char counts — O(n·k) time
# count=[0]*26; for c in word: count[ord(c)-97]+=1; key=tuple(count)

follow_up_questions: Compare sorted-string key vs char-count-tuple key — complexity and when to prefer each?
What if words could contain uppercase or special characters?
How would you also group by number of distinct characters?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Sort-as-key: O(n·k log k). Char-count-tuple: O(n·k). Key insight: canonical form as HashMap key.

algo_problems.title: Top K Frequent Elements
thinking_questions: How would you count element frequencies efficiently?
Once you have frequencies, how do you get top k without fully sorting?
The maximum frequency is n — can you use that as an array index?

hints.L1: Count frequencies first. Then think about getting the top k without sorting everything — there's a structure designed for 'give me the k largest'.
hints.L2: Count with HashMap, then use bucket sort: bucket[freq] holds all elements with that frequency. Scan from highest freq down, collect k elements.

follow_up_questions: Why does bucket sort work here but not for general sorting?
When would you prefer a heap approach over bucket sort?
What if k equals the number of unique elements?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Bucket sort: O(n). Heap: O(n log k). Key: frequency bounded by n enables bucket sort.

algo_problems.title: Product of Array Except Self
thinking_questions: output[i] = product of all left of i × product of all right of i. Can you compute these separately?
If you had a prefix product array, what would prefix[i] represent?
Can you compute in-place using only the output array and a running variable?

hints.L1: For each position you need the product of everything to its left and everything to its right — compute these two parts in two separate passes.
hints.L2: Pass 1 (left→right): fill result[i] with prefix product (product of everything before i). Pass 2 (right→left): multiply in suffix product using a running variable.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: result[i] = prefix_product[i] × suffix_product[i] — two passes, O(1) extra space
result = [1] * len(nums)
# Pass 1: prefix products
prefix = 1
for i in range(len(nums)):
    result[i] = prefix       # product of everything LEFT of i
    prefix *= nums[i]
# Pass 2: multiply suffix products in-place
suffix = 1
for i in range(len(nums)-1, -1, -1):
    result[i] *= suffix      # multiply in product of everything RIGHT of i
    suffix *= nums[i]
return result
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(1) extra (output array excluded)

follow_up_questions: If division were allowed, how would you solve it? What edge case makes division tricky?
Walk through the zero cases — what happens to prefix and suffix products?
Could you solve this in a single pass? Why or why not?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Two-pass prefix×suffix. Build prefix left→right in result[], then multiply suffix right→left in-place. O(n), O(1) extra.

algo_problems.title: Valid Sudoku
thinking_questions: Three constraints: no repeats in any row, column, or 3×3 box. How many sets do you need?
For cell (row, col), which 3×3 box does it belong to? Can you compute the box index mathematically?
The board is always 9×9 — what does that mean for your complexity?

hints.L1: You need to detect duplicates across three independent constraints simultaneously — maintain a seen-set for each row, column, and box.
hints.L2: Three arrays of 9 HashSets each: rows[9], cols[9], boxes[9]. Box index = (r//3)*3 + (c//3). Check all three sets per cell, add to all three.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: one pass, 3 constraints, 27 sets
rows = [set() for _ in range(9)]
cols = [set() for _ in range(9)]
boxes = [set() for _ in range(9)]
for r in range(9):
    for c in range(9):
        val = board[r][c]
        if val == '.': continue
        box_id = (r // 3) * 3 + (c // 3)
        if val in rows[r] or val in cols[c] or val in boxes[box_id]:
            return False
        rows[r].add(val)
        cols[c].add(val)
        boxes[box_id].add(val)
return True
# Time/Space: O(1) — fixed 9×9 board

follow_up_questions: Explain the formula (r//3)*3 + (c//3) for the box index.
How would you extend this to actually SOLVE a Sudoku puzzle?
Complexity is O(1) because input is fixed size — is that a useful answer?
algo_problems.solution_notes: 27 sets. Box index: (r//3)*3+(c//3). One pass. O(1) fixed size.

algo_problems.title: Encode and Decode Strings
thinking_questions: If you joined strings with '#', what goes wrong when a string itself contains '#'?
What if you encoded the length of each string before it?
How do protocols like HTTP handle this same framing problem?

hints.L1: A simple delimiter breaks when strings contain that delimiter — you need encoding that unambiguously marks where each string ends regardless of its content.
hints.L2: Length-prefix encoding: encode each string as '{len}#{string}'. Since you know the length, read exactly that many characters — no ambiguity.

follow_up_questions: Why doesn't '#' alone work as delimiter? Give a concrete failing example.
What other encoding strategies could work? (escaping, non-printable delimiter, netstring format)
Where else do you see length-prefix encoding in real systems?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Length-prefix: '{len}#{str}'. Decode: find '#', read length, extract that many chars. O(n).

algo_problems.title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
thinking_questions: Sorting works in O(n log n) — can you do O(n) without sorting?
For sequence [100,101,102,103], you only need to start counting from 100. How do you know 100 is the start?
What number would NOT be in the set if n were the start of a consecutive sequence?

hints.L1: O(n) means no sorting. Identify where sequences start so you only begin counting from those, not from every element.
hints.L2: Put all numbers in a HashSet. For each n, check if (n-1) is NOT in the set — if so, n is a sequence start. Count up from n while consecutive numbers exist.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: only count from sequence starts (where n-1 not in set)
# Each element visited at most twice → O(n) amortized
num_set = set(nums)
best = 0
for n in num_set:
    if (n - 1) not in num_set:  # n is a sequence start
        cur, streak = n, 1
        while (cur + 1) in num_set:
            cur += 1
            streak += 1
        best = max(best, streak)
return best
# Time: O(n) — inner while runs only for sequence starts; total steps ≤ 2n
# Space: O(n) for set

follow_up_questions: Why does checking (n-1) not in set guarantee we're at a sequence start?
Explain why the while loop doesn't make this O(n²) — what's the total number of inner iterations?
How does your solution handle duplicate numbers?
algo_problems.solution_notes: HashSet + start-only counting. Each element visited ≤2 times → O(n). Key: n-1 not in set ⟹ sequence start.

algo_problems.title: Valid Palindrome
thinking_questions: What characters should you ignore — and how do you check for alphanumeric?
If you reversed the filtered string and compared, that works — but can you avoid creating new strings?
Can you check palindrome from both ends simultaneously without extra space?

hints.L1: Compare characters from both ends while skipping non-alphanumeric characters — two pointers converging inward.
hints.L2: Left and right pointers. Skip non-alphanumeric on each side. Compare lowercased. Mismatch → false.

test_cases.input: s='A man, a plan, a canal: Panama'
s='Was it a car or a cat I saw?'

follow_up_questions: Space complexity difference between two-pointer vs filter-then-reverse?
How would you check palindrome after deleting at most one character? (LC 680)
What if case sensitivity mattered?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Two pointers, skip non-alnum, compare lowercased. O(n), O(1). Inner while loops skip invalid chars.

algo_problems.title: Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted
thinking_questions: The array is sorted — how does that change your options vs unsorted Two Sum?
If your current sum is too big, which pointer moves? Too small?
Can you do this in O(1) space (unlike Two Sum I)?

hints.L1: The array is sorted — use that. If sum is too large you need smaller numbers; too small you need larger. Think pointers at opposite ends.
hints.L2: left=0, right=end. sum>target → right--. sum<target → left++. Sorted order guarantees convergence.

follow_up_questions: Why does sorting enable O(1) space vs Two Sum I's O(n) HashMap?
Could you use binary search here? What would the complexity be?
What if there were duplicate values — does your solution still work?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Two pointers both ends. Sorted: sum too big→right--, too small→left++. O(n), O(1).

algo_problems.title: 3Sum
thinking_questions: If you fix one number, the remaining problem is Two Sum on the rest — have you solved that on a sorted array?
How do you avoid counting duplicate triplets?
After fixing nums[i], you need two numbers from the rest summing to -nums[i]. What algorithm solves that in O(n) on sorted array?

hints.L1: Fix one number in the outer loop, reduce to Two Sum II on the remaining sorted array.
hints.L2: Sort. For each i, two pointers at i+1 and end seeking -nums[i]. Skip duplicates: skip nums[i] at top of outer loop; skip left/right duplicates after finding a triplet.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: sort → fix i → two-pointer two-sum → O(n²)
nums.sort()
result = []
for i in range(len(nums) - 2):
    if i > 0 and nums[i] == nums[i-1]: continue  # skip dup i
    if nums[i] > 0: break  # sorted: can't sum to 0
    left, right = i + 1, len(nums) - 1
    while left < right:
        s = nums[i] + nums[left] + nums[right]
        if s == 0:
            result.append([nums[i], nums[left], nums[right]])
            while left < right and nums[left] == nums[left+1]: left += 1
            while left < right and nums[right] == nums[right-1]: right -= 1
            left += 1; right -= 1
        elif s < 0: left += 1
        else: right -= 1
return result
# Time: O(n²)  Space: O(1) excluding output

follow_up_questions: Why skip i-duplicates at the top of the loop but l/r-duplicates only AFTER finding a match?
Why is the early break `if nums[i] > 0` valid? What property makes it safe?
Can you generalize to 4Sum? What's the complexity of k-Sum generally?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Sort + fix i + two-pointer. O(n²). Three duplicate-skip points. Key: sorted array + i>0 early break.

algo_problems.title: Container With Most Water
thinking_questions: Area = min(h[l], h[r]) × (r-l). Width always decreases when you move a pointer — when is it worth it?
Moving the taller line inward — can area ever increase? What about the shorter line?
Which pointer should you move to have any chance of increasing area?

hints.L1: Width decreases with every pointer move — only move when there's a chance height increases enough. Think about which pointer to move.
hints.L2: Always move the pointer pointing to the shorter height. Moving the taller can only decrease or maintain area; moving the shorter gives a chance of finding a taller line.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: area limited by shorter height — moving shorter gives chance to improve
left, right = 0, len(height) - 1
best = 0
while left < right:
    area = min(height[left], height[right]) * (right - left)
    best = max(best, area)
    if height[left] <= height[right]:
        left += 1
    else:
        right -= 1
return best
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(1)
# Proof: moving taller → area ≤ min(shorter,new) × (narrower width) ≤ current

follow_up_questions: Prove why moving the shorter pointer is always correct.
What if you needed the minimum water container instead of maximum?
This is greedy — what's the greedy choice and why is it safe?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Greedy two-pointer: always move shorter side. O(n), O(1). Key proof: moving taller → bounded by shorter × narrower.

algo_problems.title: Trapping Rain Water
thinking_questions: Water at i = min(max_left, max_right) - height[i]. How do you know both maxes efficiently?
You could precompute left_max[] and right_max[] arrays — what's that approach's complexity?
Can you compute both maxes simultaneously with two pointers, avoiding those arrays?

hints.L1: At each position, water = min(tallest to the left, tallest to the right) - height[i]. Think about efficiently knowing both maxes.
hints.L2: Two-pointer approach: maintain left_max and right_max. Process from the side with the smaller max — that side's water level is fully determined.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: process smaller-max side first — it's the bottleneck
left, right = 0, len(height) - 1
left_max, right_max = 0, 0
water = 0
while left < right:
    if left_max <= right_max:
        left_max = max(left_max, height[left])
        water += left_max - height[left]  # left_max is the limiting factor
        left += 1
    else:
        right_max = max(right_max, height[right])
        water += right_max - height[right]
        right -= 1
return water
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(1)
# Why safe: when left_max≤right_max, water at left is exactly left_max-height[left]
# because right_max ≥ left_max, so left_max is the min

follow_up_questions: When left_max≤right_max, why is the water at left guaranteed to be left_max-height[left]?
Walk through the precompute arrays approach then explain how two pointers reduce space.
How would the monotonic stack approach work? When might you prefer it?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Two-pointer: process smaller-max side. O(n), O(1). Key: smaller max is the water ceiling; other side ≥ it.

algo_problems.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
thinking_questions: You must buy before sell. At day i, the best you could have bought is the minimum price seen so far — how do you track that?
At each day, best profit = today's price - minimum price seen so far. One pass?
What if prices only decrease? What should you return?

hints.L1: Scan forward tracking the lowest price seen — best profit at any day is today's price minus that minimum.
hints.L2: One pass: maintain min_price. At each day compute profit=price-min_price, update best, then update min_price.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: at each day, optimal buy = global min of all previous prices
min_price = prices[0]
best_profit = 0
for price in prices:
    best_profit = max(best_profit, price - min_price)
    min_price = min(min_price, price)
return best_profit
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(1)

follow_up_questions: What if you could make unlimited transactions? (LC 122)
What if you could make at most 2 transactions? (LC 123) — how does state change?
Why is this considered a sliding window problem? What is the window?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Track running min_price, compute profit each step. O(n), O(1). Base case for stock problem series.

algo_problems.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
thinking_questions: What makes the window invalid here?
When you hit a duplicate, can you jump left directly instead of shrinking one step?
If you store the last-seen index of each char, how quickly can you move left?

hints.L1: Sliding window — expand right until a repeat, then shrink from the left. The key is how efficiently you find where to shrink to.
hints.L2: Store each char's last-seen index in a HashMap. On repeat, jump left to max(left, last_seen[char]+1).
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: HashMap of last index → jump left pointer past previous occurrence
char_index = {}
left = 0
best = 0
for right, char in enumerate(s):
    if char in char_index and char_index[char] >= left:
        left = char_index[char] + 1  # jump past previous
    char_index[char] = right
    best = max(best, right - left + 1)
return best
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(1) — at most 128 ASCII chars
# Why >= left: prevents moving left backward if char was seen before window start

follow_up_questions: Why is `char_index[char] >= left` necessary? What case does it handle?
Compare jumping left vs shrinking one step — both O(n), but why?
What if the problem asked for at most k distinct characters? (LC 340)
algo_problems.solution_notes: Sliding window + char→last_index map. Jump left past prev occurrence. O(n), O(1). Key: >= left check.

algo_problems.title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
thinking_questions: In a window with max_freq dominant chars, how many replacements are needed?
What condition makes a window valid (replaceable in ≤k steps)?
When the window becomes invalid do you need to update max_freq as you shrink?

hints.L1: A window is valid if (size - most_frequent_char_count) ≤ k. Expand freely, shrink when invalid.
hints.L2: Track max_freq of any char in window. Invalid when (right-left+1)-max_freq > k. Shrink left by 1.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: valid ⟺ (window_size - max_freq) ≤ k
# Never need to shrink max_freq — window only grows or stays same size
count = {}
left = 0
max_freq = 0
best = 0
for right in range(len(s)):
    count[s[right]] = count.get(s[right], 0) + 1
    max_freq = max(max_freq, count[s[right]])
    if (right - left + 1) - max_freq > k:
        count[s[left]] -= 1
        left += 1  # shrink by 1; don't update max_freq
    best = max(best, right - left + 1)
return best
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(1)

follow_up_questions: Why is it safe not to update max_freq when shrinking?
The max_freq can be 'stale' (too high) — why doesn't this break correctness?
How would this change if you could replace with any character to maximize uniqueness?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Sliding window. Valid if (size-max_freq)≤k. Never decrease max_freq. O(n), O(1).

algo_problems.title: Permutation in String
thinking_questions: A permutation of s1 has the same char frequencies as s1. What window size in s2 should you look at?
With a fixed-size window, how do you efficiently update frequencies as it slides?
Can you track a 'matches' counter instead of comparing full frequency arrays each step?

hints.L1: Fixed-size sliding window of length len(s1) in s2 — check if any window has identical frequencies to s1.
hints.L2: Two frequency arrays, slide the window. Track 'matches' counter (chars where freq matches) to avoid O(26) comparison each step.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: fixed window size=len(s1); track matches count → O(1) per step
if len(s1) > len(s2): return False
c1, c2 = [0]*26, [0]*26
for c in s1: c1[ord(c)-97] += 1
matches = sum(1 for i in range(26) if c1[i]==c2[i])
for i in range(len(s2)):
    r = ord(s2[i])-97
    if c1[r]==c2[r]: matches -= 1
    c2[r] += 1
    if c1[r]==c2[r]: matches += 1
    if i >= len(s1):
        l = ord(s2[i-len(s1)])-97
        if c1[l]==c2[l]: matches -= 1
        c2[l] -= 1
        if c1[l]==c2[l]: matches += 1
    if matches == 26: return True
return False
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(1)

follow_up_questions: Advantage of matches counter vs comparing full 26-char arrays each step?
How does this differ from Minimum Window Substring structurally?
Could you solve this with sorting? What's the complexity?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Fixed window=len(s1). 'matches' counter for O(1) comparison per step. O(n), O(1).

algo_problems.title: Minimum Window Substring
thinking_questions: What makes a window valid? How do you track 'all chars of t are covered'?
Once valid, should you keep expanding or start shrinking?
How do you check 'window contains all of t' in O(1) without scanning the window?

hints.L1: Variable-size window. Expand right until valid (contains all of t), then shrink left to find minimum. Repeat.
hints.L2: t_count map + window map + 'have'/'need' counters. 'have' increments when a char hits its required freq. When have==need, shrink.

follow_up_questions: Why count 'have' based on DISTINCT satisfied chars, not total matched chars?
Key structural difference vs Permutation in String (fixed vs variable window)?
If t has duplicates like 'AAB', how does your solution ensure enough of each?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Variable window. have/need counters. Expand right, shrink left when valid. O(n).

algo_problems.title: Sliding Window Maximum
thinking_questions: Brute force: scan every window of size k. O(n·k). Can you avoid re-scanning?
If nums[i] < nums[j] with i<j, can nums[i] ever be the max of any future window?
What structure lets you get the max in O(1) and remove out-of-window elements?

hints.L1: When a new larger element enters, all previous smaller elements can never be max again — maintain a 'useful candidates' list.
hints.L2: Monotonic decreasing deque of indices. Add new element: pop back all smaller elements. Pop front if index out of window. Front = window max.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: monotonic decreasing deque — front always = window max
from collections import deque
dq = deque()  # stores indices
result = []
for i, num in enumerate(nums):
    while dq and dq[0] < i-k+1: dq.popleft()  # remove out-of-window
    while dq and nums[dq[-1]] < num: dq.pop()  # remove smaller (useless)
    dq.append(i)
    if i >= k-1: result.append(nums[dq[0]])    # window full
return result
# Time: O(n) — each element added/removed at most once
# Space: O(k)

follow_up_questions: Why monotonically decreasing? What breaks if you allow increasing?
Each element pushed/popped at most once — why does this make it O(n) not O(n·k)?
What other problems use monotonic deque/stack? (Daily Temperatures, Largest Rectangle)
algo_problems.solution_notes: Monotonic decreasing deque of indices. Front=max. O(n), O(k).

algo_problems.title: Valid Parentheses
thinking_questions: When you see a closing bracket, what do you need to know to check if it's valid?
You need to match the most recently seen unmatched opening bracket — what data structure gives you the most recent?
What are the three failure conditions?

hints.L1: When you encounter a closing bracket, you need to match it with the most recently unmatched opening bracket — think about which data structure gives you 'most recent'.
hints.L2: Stack. Push opening brackets. On closing bracket, pop from stack and check if it matches. Three failure cases: mismatch, stack empty on close, stack non-empty at end.

follow_up_questions: What are the exactly three ways this can fail?
What if the string had only one type of bracket? How would your approach simplify?
How would you handle wildcards where '*' can be '(', ')' or empty? (LC 678)
algo_problems.solution_notes: Stack: push opens, pop on close and verify match. Three failure modes: mismatch, empty stack on close, non-empty at end.

algo_problems.title: Min Stack
thinking_questions: getMin in O(1) seems to require knowing the minimum at every possible stack state — how do you store that?
The minimum only changes when you push something smaller or pop the current minimum — can you track min per stack level?
What if you stored (value, current_min) as a pair at each stack level?

hints.L1: You need O(1) getMin — that means you can't scan the stack. Think about storing extra information at each stack level.
hints.L2: Store pairs: (value, min_at_this_level). On push, new_min = min(val, current_min). On getMin, return top pair's min.

follow_up_questions: What if instead of pairs, you used a separate auxiliary stack tracking only minimums?
Does your solution handle pushing the same minimum value twice correctly?
How would you extend this to also support getMax in O(1)?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Store (value, running_min) pairs. Every level knows the min at that point. All ops O(1).

algo_problems.title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
thinking_questions: In RPN, operators always apply to the two most recently seen operands — what data structure gives you 'most recently seen'?
When you see an operator, how many operands do you need and where are they?
What does integer division truncating toward zero mean? How do you implement that in Python?

hints.L1: In RPN an operator always applies to the two most recently encountered numbers — think about which structure gives you constant-time access to the most recent items.
hints.L2: Stack. Push numbers. When you see an operator, pop two numbers, apply the operator, push the result. Final answer is the only remaining element.

follow_up_questions: Why does pop order matter — why is `b, a = stack.pop(), stack.pop()` correct?
What does 'truncate toward zero' mean for division, and how does Python's // differ?
How would you convert an infix expression to RPN? (Shunting-yard algorithm)
algo_problems.solution_notes: Stack: push numbers, operators pop two and push result. Watch pop order for subtraction/division. int(a/b) for truncate-toward-zero.

algo_problems.title: Generate Parentheses
thinking_questions: When can you add an open bracket? When can you add a closing bracket?
How do you know a partial string is heading toward a valid combination vs invalid?
What are the two constraints that determine when each bracket type is valid?

hints.L1: Build the string character by character. At each step you can add '(' or ')' — but only under certain conditions. Think about when each is valid.
hints.L2: Backtrack: add '(' if open_count < n; add ')' if close_count < open_count. Recurse. Base case: string length == 2n.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: add '(' if opens remaining; add ')' if close_count < open_count
result = []
def backtrack(cur, open_count, close_count):
    if len(cur) == 2*n:
        result.append(cur)
        return
    if open_count < n:
        backtrack(cur+'(', open_count+1, close_count)
    if close_count < open_count:
        backtrack(cur+')', open_count, close_count+1)
backtrack('', 0, 0)
return result
# Time: O(4^n/√n) — Catalan number of valid combos
# Space: O(n) recursion depth

follow_up_questions: How many valid combinations exist for n pairs? What's the Catalan number formula?
Why does `close_count < open_count` guarantee we never close more than we open?
How would you generate all valid combinations iteratively using a stack?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Backtracking. Add '(' if opens<n; add ')' if close<open. O(4^n/√n) = nth Catalan number combos.

algo_problems.title: Daily Temperatures
thinking_questions: For each day you need the next day that's warmer — brute force scans right for each index. Can you avoid redundant scanning?
If you keep a list of 'waiting' days that haven't found a warmer day yet, when do you resolve them?
When you see a warmer day, which waiting days can you resolve?

hints.L1: For each temperature, you need the next higher one. Instead of scanning right for each, think about processing left-to-right and resolving waiting days when you find something warmer.
hints.L2: Monotonic decreasing stack of indices. For each new temp, pop stack while top is cooler — answer for popped index = current_index - popped_index.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: monotonic decreasing stack — pop when warmer found
stack = []  # indices with no warmer day found yet
result = [0] * len(temperatures)
for i, temp in enumerate(temperatures):
    while stack and temperatures[stack[-1]] < temp:
        j = stack.pop()
        result[j] = i - j  # days waited
    stack.append(i)
return result
# Time: O(n) — each index pushed/popped once
# Space: O(n)

follow_up_questions: Why is the stack monotonically decreasing? What does that mean for the values in it?
Each element pushed/popped once — why does this give O(n) total?
How does this relate to the 'next greater element' pattern?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Monotonic decreasing stack of indices. Pop on warmer temperature, compute gap. O(n).

algo_problems.title: Car Fleet
thinking_questions: A car can only catch another if it's behind it. What order should you process cars?
If car A would reach the target after car B, can A ever catch B? What happens?
Time-to-target for each car = (target-position)/speed. What does it mean when one car's time ≤ another's?

hints.L1: Process cars from closest-to-target to farthest. A car catches up to a fleet if its time-to-target is ≤ the fleet ahead of it.
hints.L2: Sort by position descending. Compute time = (target-pos)/speed for each. Use a stack: push each time; if top ≤ previous top, they merge (pop, it joins the fleet ahead).

follow_up_questions: Why does processing from closest-to-target first make sense?
What does it mean when two cars form a fleet — which one's time determines the fleet's arrival?
How does this relate to finding 'next greater element' conceptually?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Sort by pos descending. time=(target-pos)/speed. Stack: push if time>top, else merges into fleet ahead. O(n log n).

algo_problems.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
thinking_questions: For each bar, the rectangle it anchors extends as far left and right as bars are at least as tall. How do you find those boundaries efficiently?
If you maintain a stack of increasing heights, what can you determine when a shorter bar arrives?
When you pop a bar from a decreasing stack because a shorter bar arrived, what are the left and right boundaries of that bar's rectangle?

hints.L1: For each bar, its max rectangle extends left and right until hitting a shorter bar. Think about processing bars and maintaining a structure that tracks the left boundary.
hints.L2: Monotonic increasing stack (store indices). When current bar is shorter than stack top, pop: the popped bar's rectangle has right=current index, left=new stack top. width = right-left-1.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: monotonic increasing stack; pop when shorter bar arrives → compute width
stack = []  # (index, height) — monotonically increasing heights
best = 0
for i, h in enumerate(heights):
    start = i
    while stack and stack[-1][1] > h:
        idx, height = stack.pop()
        # rectangle with this height extends from idx to current
        best = max(best, height * (i - idx))
        start = idx  # can extend further left
    stack.append((start, h))
# Process remaining bars in stack
for idx, height in stack:
    best = max(best, height * (len(heights) - idx))
return best
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(n)

follow_up_questions: Explain why 'start=idx' in the loop allows the popped bar's rectangle to extend leftward.
What happens to bars still in the stack when you finish iterating — why do they extend to the right edge?
How does this relate to Trapping Rain Water conceptually?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Monotonic increasing stack with (start_index, height). Pop on shorter bar → compute area. Remaining stack extends to right edge. O(n).

algo_problems.title: Binary Search
thinking_questions: The array is sorted. What information does comparing to the middle element give you?
After each comparison you can eliminate half the array — how does this lead to O(log n)?
What's the loop invariant — what's always true about left and right?

hints.L1: Sorted array: compare to the middle element, then eliminate the half that can't contain the target.
hints.L2: left=0, right=n-1. mid=(left+right)//2. If nums[mid]==target return mid. If nums[mid]<target, left=mid+1. Else right=mid-1.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: each step eliminates half the search space → O(log n)
left, right = 0, len(nums) - 1
while left <= right:
    mid = left + (right - left) // 2  # avoids overflow vs (l+r)//2
    if nums[mid] == target: return mid
    elif nums[mid] < target: left = mid + 1
    else: right = mid - 1
return -1
# Time: O(log n)  Space: O(1)
# Note: left+(right-left)//2 vs (left+right)//2 — identical in Python, but safe in C++

follow_up_questions: Why use left+(right-left)//2 instead of (left+right)//2? When does it matter?
What's the invariant — what's always true about nums[left..right] when the loop runs?
When does `while left <= right` terminate vs `while left < right`? When would you use each?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Classic binary search. mid=left+(right-left)//2. left<=right loop. O(log n), O(1).

algo_problems.title: Search a 2D Matrix
thinking_questions: Each row is sorted and the first element of each row is greater than the last of the previous — what does this tell you about the entire matrix?
If you 'unrolled' the matrix into a single sorted array, how would you map a 1D index back to (row, col)?
Can you binary search the entire matrix as if it were one sorted array?

hints.L1: The matrix is essentially one sorted array laid out in rows — you can binary search on the virtual 1D index.
hints.L2: Binary search on range [0, m*n-1]. Map mid to (mid//n, mid%n) to get the cell value.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: treat m×n matrix as 1D sorted array; map index → (row, col)
m, n = len(matrix), len(matrix[0])
left, right = 0, m*n - 1
while left <= right:
    mid = left + (right - left) // 2
    val = matrix[mid // n][mid % n]
    if val == target: return True
    elif val < target: left = mid + 1
    else: right = mid - 1
return False
# Time: O(log(m·n))  Space: O(1)

follow_up_questions: Explain the mapping mid//n and mid%n — why does this work?
What if the matrix were NOT fully sorted (each row sorted but first of row < last of previous row not guaranteed)? (LC 240)
Could you do two separate binary searches (first find row, then column) instead? Same complexity?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Map 1D binary search to 2D: row=mid//n, col=mid%n. O(log(m·n)), O(1).

algo_problems.title: Koko Eating Bananas
thinking_questions: As eating speed increases, hours needed decreases — what does this monotone relationship suggest?
What's the search space? What are the minimum and maximum possible speeds?
Can you binary search on the speed rather than on the piles?

hints.L1: The answer is a speed value. As speed increases, hours decrease monotonically — this suggests binary search on the answer.
hints.L2: Binary search on speed [1..max(piles)]. For each speed, compute total hours = sum(ceil(pile/speed)). Find minimum speed where total_hours ≤ h.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: binary search on ANSWER (speed) — monotone feasibility function
import math
left, right = 1, max(piles)
result = right
while left <= right:
    mid = left + (right - left) // 2
    hours = sum(math.ceil(pile / mid) for pile in piles)
    if hours <= h:
        result = mid  # feasible, try smaller speed
        right = mid - 1
    else:
        left = mid + 1  # too slow, need higher speed
return result
# Time: O(n log m) where m=max(piles)  Space: O(1)

follow_up_questions: How do you recognize 'binary search on the answer' problems? What's the key property?
Why is max(piles) the upper bound? What's the lower bound and why 1?
What other problems fit the 'binary search on answer' template? (Capacity to Ship, Split Array)
algo_problems.solution_notes: Binary search on speed [1..max(piles)]. feasible(speed) = sum(ceil(p/s)) ≤ h. O(n log m).

algo_problems.title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
thinking_questions: The array is two sorted subarrays joined. The minimum is at the join point. How do you find it?
If nums[mid] > nums[right], which half contains the minimum?
What's the invariant that tells you which half to search?

hints.L1: The rotated array consists of two sorted halves. Compare mid to right to determine which half is 'out of order' — that's where the minimum is.
hints.L2: If nums[mid] > nums[right]: min is in right half (left=mid+1). Else: min is in left half including mid (right=mid).

follow_up_questions: Why compare mid to right rather than to left?
What if the array had duplicate values? How does that break the approach? (LC 154)
How would you find the number of rotations (the rotation index)?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Compare nums[mid] to nums[right]. mid>right → min in right half. O(log n), O(1).

algo_problems.title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
thinking_questions: The array is two sorted halves — in any binary search step, at least one half is guaranteed to be fully sorted. Which one?
If the left half is sorted, how do you know if target is in it?
How do you decide which half to recurse into given one is sorted and one might not be?

hints.L1: At any mid point, at least one side is fully sorted. Determine which side is sorted, check if target falls in it, and eliminate the other half.
hints.L2: If nums[left]≤nums[mid]: left half is sorted. Check if target in [nums[left],nums[mid]]. If yes, go left; else go right. Otherwise right half is sorted — do the same.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: one side always sorted → determine which, check if target in range
left, right = 0, len(nums)-1
while left <= right:
    mid = left + (right-left)//2
    if nums[mid] == target: return mid
    if nums[left] <= nums[mid]:  # left half is sorted
        if nums[left] <= target < nums[mid]:
            right = mid-1
        else:
            left = mid+1
    else:  # right half is sorted
        if nums[mid] < target <= nums[right]:
            left = mid+1
        else:
            right = mid-1
return -1
# Time: O(log n)  Space: O(1)

follow_up_questions: Why does `nums[left] <= nums[mid]` indicate the left half is sorted?
What edge case does the `<=` (rather than `<`) in `nums[left] <= nums[mid]` handle?
What changes if duplicates are allowed? (LC 81 — can you always determine which side is sorted?)
algo_problems.solution_notes: One side always sorted. Check which, test if target in range. O(log n), O(1). Key: <= handles left=mid=single element.

algo_problems.title: Time Based Key-Value Store
thinking_questions: Timestamps are strictly increasing — what does that tell you about the list of values for a key?
To find the largest timestamp ≤ t, what search strategy works on a sorted list?
What do you store per key and what do you binary search on?

hints.L1: Timestamps are always increasing on set — so per key, timestamps are sorted. Binary search for the largest timestamp ≤ t.
hints.L2: HashMap: key → list of (timestamp, value). On get, binary search for largest timestamp ≤ t. Return '' if none found.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: sorted timestamps per key → binary search for largest ≤ t
from collections import defaultdict
store = defaultdict(list)  # key → [(timestamp, value)]

def set(key, value, timestamp):
    store[key].append((timestamp, value))  # always increasing

def get(key, timestamp):
    if key not in store: return ''
    pairs = store[key]
    left, right = 0, len(pairs)-1
    result = ''
    while left <= right:
        mid = left + (right-left)//2
        if pairs[mid][0] <= timestamp:
            result = pairs[mid][1]  # valid candidate, try later
            left = mid+1
        else:
            right = mid-1
    return result
# set: O(1)  get: O(log n)

follow_up_questions: Why are timestamps guaranteed sorted per key? What property of set() ensures this?
What if the same key was set at timestamp 5, and you query at timestamp 3 — what should return?
How would you handle the case where timestamps could be non-monotonic (set could be called out of order)?
algo_problems.solution_notes: HashMap key→[(ts,val)]. Timestamps sorted (guaranteed by problem). Binary search for largest ts≤t. O(1) set, O(log n) get.

algo_problems.title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
thinking_questions: The median splits the combined array into two equal halves. How many elements from each array should be in the left half?
If you pick i elements from nums1 and j=(half-i) elements from nums2, when is this a valid partition?
What's the binary search invariant — what are you searching for?

hints.L1: You're partitioning both arrays such that the left half of nums1 and left half of nums2 together form the left half of the merged array. Binary search for the right partition point.
hints.L2: Binary search on nums1 (smaller array). For each partition i in nums1, j=(half-i) in nums2. Valid when nums1[i-1]≤nums2[j] and nums2[j-1]≤nums1[i].
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: binary search for partition where left halves correctly split merged array
if len(nums1) > len(nums2): nums1, nums2 = nums2, nums1  # ensure nums1 shorter
m, n = len(nums1), len(nums2)
half = (m+n)//2
left, right = 0, m-1
while True:
    i = left+(right-left)//2  # partition nums1
    j = half-i-2               # partition nums2
    n1_left  = nums1[i]   if i >= 0 else float('-inf')
    n1_right = nums1[i+1] if i+1 < m else float('inf')
    n2_left  = nums2[j]   if j >= 0 else float('-inf')
    n2_right = nums2[j+1] if j+1 < n else float('inf')
    if n1_left <= n2_right and n2_left <= n1_right:
        if (m+n) % 2: return min(n1_right, n2_right)
        return (max(n1_left,n2_left)+min(n1_right,n2_right))/2
    elif n1_left > n2_right: right = i-1
    else: left = i+1
# Time: O(log(min(m,n)))  Space: O(1)

follow_up_questions: Why do we binary search on the SMALLER array?
Explain the partition invariant — what condition defines a valid partition?
If O(m+n) is acceptable, how would you solve this with a two-pointer merge approach?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Binary search on smaller array partition. Valid partition: n1_left≤n2_right and n2_left≤n1_right. O(log(min(m,n))).

algo_problems.title: Reverse Linked List
thinking_questions: To reverse a pointer, you need to know the previous node — but a singly linked list doesn't store that. How do you track it?
How many pointers do you need to manipulate at each step to reverse the link without losing the rest of the list?
Can you do it recursively? What does the recursive call return and what do you do with it?

hints.L1: You need to flip each node's next pointer to point backwards. You'll need to track the previous node since singly linked lists only go forward.
hints.L2: Three pointers: prev=None, curr=head, next=None. At each step: save next, flip curr.next=prev, advance prev=curr, curr=next.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: three pointers — save next before flipping
prev, curr = None, head
while curr:
    next_node = curr.next  # save before overwriting
    curr.next = prev        # flip the pointer
    prev = curr             # advance prev
    curr = next_node        # advance curr
return prev  # prev is new head
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(1)
# Recursive: def rev(node): if not node.next: return node
#             new_head=rev(node.next); node.next.next=node; node.next=None; return new_head

follow_up_questions: Implement the recursive version. What's the space complexity difference?
How would you reverse only a portion of the list from position left to right? (LC 92)
What happens if you flip curr.next=prev BEFORE saving curr.next?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Three pointers iterative: prev/curr/next. O(n), O(1). Recursive: O(n) space for call stack.

algo_problems.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
thinking_questions: You need to pick the smaller of the two current heads at each step. Then what?
How do you handle the leftover tail when one list runs out?
A dummy head node simplifies edge cases — how does it help?

hints.L1: Compare the two current heads and append the smaller one to your result. Advance that list's pointer. Attach the remaining tail at the end.
hints.L2: Use a dummy head node to simplify edge cases. Pointer 'curr' advances the result list. After the loop, attach whichever list still has nodes.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: dummy head eliminates edge cases; append smaller each step
dummy = ListNode(0)
curr = dummy
while list1 and list2:
    if list1.val <= list2.val:
        curr.next = list1
        list1 = list1.next
    else:
        curr.next = list2
        list2 = list2.next
    curr = curr.next
curr.next = list1 or list2  # attach remaining tail
return dummy.next
# Time: O(m+n)  Space: O(1)

follow_up_questions: Why is the dummy head helpful? What edge case does it simplify?
What's the recursive implementation? What's the space complexity vs iterative?
How would you extend this to merge k sorted lists? (LC 23)
algo_problems.solution_notes: Dummy head + compare and append smaller. Attach remaining tail. O(m+n), O(1).

algo_problems.title: Reorder List
thinking_questions: The pattern is: first, last, second, second-to-last... The second half needs to be reversed and merged with the first. How do you split the list in half?
Once you have the first half and reversed second half, how do you interleave them?
What three operations are needed and in what order?

hints.L1: Break this into three steps: find the middle (slow/fast pointers), reverse the second half, then merge the two halves.
hints.L2: 1) Find mid with slow/fast pointers. 2) Reverse second half in-place. 3) Interleave: first→second_first→first_next→...
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: find mid → reverse second half → interleave
# Step 1: find middle
slow, fast = head, head.next
while fast and fast.next:
    slow = slow.next
    fast = fast.next.next
second = slow.next
slow.next = None  # split list
# Step 2: reverse second half
prev, curr = None, second
while curr:
    nxt = curr.next
    curr.next = prev
    prev, curr = curr, nxt
second = prev
# Step 3: interleave
first = head
while second:
    tmp1, tmp2 = first.next, second.next
    first.next = second
    second.next = tmp1
    first, second = tmp1, tmp2
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(1)

follow_up_questions: Why does slow/fast pointer approach find the correct midpoint for even vs odd length lists?
What goes wrong if you forget to set slow.next=None after splitting?
How would the approach change for a doubly linked list?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Three steps: find mid (slow/fast), reverse second half, interleave. O(n), O(1).

algo_problems.title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
thinking_questions: Naive: find length, then traverse to (len-n)th node. Can you do it in one pass?
If two pointers start n+1 apart, what's the relationship between them when the fast pointer reaches the end?
How does a dummy head simplify removing the first node?

hints.L1: Use two pointers n+1 nodes apart. When the fast pointer reaches the end, the slow pointer is right before the node to remove.
hints.L2: Dummy head simplifies edge case of removing head. Advance fast n+1 times. Then advance both until fast is None. slow.next = slow.next.next.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: gap of n+1 between pointers → slow lands before target node
dummy = ListNode(0, head)
left = dummy
right = head
for _ in range(n):  # advance right n steps
    right = right.next
while right:  # advance both until right is None
    left = left.next
    right = right.next
left.next = left.next.next  # remove nth from end
return dummy.next
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(1)

follow_up_questions: Why advance fast n steps (not n-1)? Draw it out for a list of 5 nodes, n=2.
Why does the dummy head simplify the case of removing the first node?
What if n were larger than the list length? How would you handle that?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Dummy head + two pointers with gap n. Advance fast n times, then both until fast=None. Remove slow.next. O(n), O(1).

algo_problems.title: Copy List with Random Pointer
thinking_questions: Random pointers can point anywhere, including forward — why can't you just copy one pass and set random pointers immediately?
If you first create all new nodes, then set pointers, how do you map old nodes to their new copies?
Can you solve this in O(1) extra space by interleaving old and new nodes?

hints.L1: Random pointers may point to nodes you haven't created yet — so create all copies first, then set the pointers in a second pass.
hints.L2: Two passes: Pass 1: HashMap {old_node → new_node}, create all copies. Pass 2: set new_node.next and new_node.random using the HashMap.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: HashMap old→new allows setting random pointers in second pass
if not head: return None
old_to_new = {}  # old_node → new_node
# Pass 1: create all nodes
curr = head
while curr:
    old_to_new[curr] = Node(curr.val)
    curr = curr.next
# Pass 2: set next and random
curr = head
while curr:
    if curr.next: old_to_new[curr].next = old_to_new[curr.next]
    if curr.random: old_to_new[curr].random = old_to_new[curr.random]
    curr = curr.next
return old_to_new[head]
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(n)

follow_up_questions: How would you solve this in O(1) extra space by weaving new nodes into the original list?
Why does the two-pass approach work when single-pass with immediate pointer setting doesn't?
What if nodes could have multiple random pointers?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Two-pass: create all nodes with HashMap, then set pointers. O(n) time/space. O(1) space alt: interleave new nodes in original list.

algo_problems.title: Add Two Numbers
thinking_questions: Numbers are stored in reverse — does that simplify or complicate addition?
What are the three sources of a digit in the result: two input digits and what else?
What do you do if the lists are different lengths? What if there's a final carry?

hints.L1: Simulate grade-school addition: add digits position by position, carry the overflow. The reverse storage actually helps — least significant digit is at the head.
hints.L2: Dummy head. Iterate while either list has nodes or carry > 0. Sum = l1.val + l2.val + carry. New digit = sum%10. Carry = sum//10.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: simulate addition with carry; reverse order makes it natural
dummy = ListNode(0)
curr = dummy
carry = 0
while l1 or l2 or carry:
    v1 = l1.val if l1 else 0
    v2 = l2.val if l2 else 0
    total = v1 + v2 + carry
    carry = total // 10
    curr.next = ListNode(total % 10)
    curr = curr.next
    if l1: l1 = l1.next
    if l2: l2 = l2.next
return dummy.next
# Time: O(max(m,n))  Space: O(max(m,n))

follow_up_questions: Why is the loop condition `while l1 or l2 or carry` — what case does `or carry` handle?
If numbers were stored forward (most significant first), how would the approach change?
What's the maximum number of nodes in the result? (max(m,n)+1 — when?)
algo_problems.solution_notes: Simulate addition with carry. Loop while l1 or l2 or carry. Digit=sum%10, carry=sum//10. O(max(m,n)).

algo_problems.title: Linked List Cycle
thinking_questions: If there's a cycle, what happens when you follow next pointers forever?
If two runners move at different speeds in a cycle, do they eventually meet?
Can you detect a cycle without storing visited nodes (O(1) space)?

hints.L1: If there's a cycle, a fast pointer lapping a slow pointer will eventually catch up. If no cycle, the fast pointer will hit None.
hints.L2: Floyd's algorithm: slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2. If they meet, there's a cycle. If fast reaches None, no cycle.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: Floyd's cycle detection — fast catches slow in a cycle
slow, fast = head, head
while fast and fast.next:
    slow = slow.next
    fast = fast.next.next
    if slow == fast: return True
return False
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(1)
# HashSet approach: O(n) time, O(n) space — simpler but less optimal

follow_up_questions: Prove why fast always catches slow — why can't fast 'skip over' slow?
How do you find the START of the cycle, not just detect it? (Floyd's part 2 — LC 142)
When would you prefer the HashSet approach over Floyd's?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Floyd's two-pointer: slow+1, fast+2. Meet → cycle. No meet → no cycle. O(n), O(1).

algo_problems.title: Find the Duplicate Number
thinking_questions: Values are in range [1,n] and indices are [0,n] — if you treat each value as a 'next pointer', what structure does that create?
If there's a duplicate, what does that mean in terms of the 'linked list' created by using values as indices?
Isn't this just cycle detection on a virtual linked list?

hints.L1: Think of each nums[i] as a 'pointer' to index nums[i]. The duplicate value creates two pointers into the same node — meaning a cycle.
hints.L2: Floyd's cycle detection on the virtual linked list (i → nums[i]). Phase 1: find meeting point. Phase 2: find cycle start (the duplicate).
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: treat nums as linked list i→nums[i]; duplicate = cycle entry
# Phase 1: find intersection
slow, fast = nums[0], nums[nums[0]]
while slow != fast:
    slow = nums[slow]
    fast = nums[nums[fast]]
# Phase 2: find cycle entry (duplicate)
slow2 = 0
while slow != slow2:
    slow = nums[slow]
    slow2 = nums[slow2]
return slow
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(1)  — cannot modify array, without extra space

follow_up_questions: Explain why the duplicate creates a cycle in the virtual linked list.
Why can't you just sort or use a HashSet here (given the constraints)?
Explain Floyd's phase 2 — why does starting a second slow pointer from 0 find the duplicate?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Floyd's cycle detection on virtual linked list (index→value). Phase 1: find meeting. Phase 2: from 0 find entry=duplicate. O(n), O(1).

algo_problems.title: LRU Cache
thinking_questions: You need O(1) get and O(1) put with LRU eviction. What two data structures combined achieve this?
For O(1) eviction of the least recently used, you need to quickly remove from the tail — which list structure supports O(1) removal anywhere?
How does a HashMap pointing to linked list nodes enable O(1) operations for both lookup and order maintenance?

hints.L1: Combine a HashMap (O(1) lookup) with a doubly linked list (O(1) insertion/deletion anywhere). The list maintains LRU order.
hints.L2: Doubly linked list with dummy head and tail. HashMap: key → node. On access/update, move node to front (most recent). On eviction, remove from tail (least recent).

follow_up_questions: Why doubly linked list and not singly? What operation requires backward traversal?
Why two dummy nodes (head and tail)? What edge cases do they eliminate?
How would you implement LFU (Least Frequently Used) cache? (LC 460)
algo_problems.solution_notes: HashMap + doubly linked list. Dummies at head/tail. Move to front on access. Evict from tail. All ops O(1).

algo_problems.title: Merge K Sorted Lists
thinking_questions: You need the global minimum across k list heads at each step — how do you find that efficiently?
A min-heap of size k gives you the minimum in O(log k) — how do you use it here?
Can you solve this with divide-and-conquer by merging pairs of lists?

hints.L1: At each step you need the minimum of the k current heads. A min-heap gives you that in O(log k) instead of O(k).
hints.L2: Push all k heads into a min-heap. Pop the minimum, append to result, push its next node. Repeat until heap empty.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: min-heap tracks k current heads → O(log k) per extraction
import heapq
dummy = ListNode(0); curr = dummy
heap = []
for i, node in enumerate(lists):
    if node: heapq.heappush(heap, (node.val, i, node))
while heap:
    val, i, node = heapq.heappop(heap)
    curr.next = node; curr = curr.next
    if node.next: heapq.heappush(heap, (node.next.val, i, node.next))
return dummy.next
# Time: O(n log k) where n=total nodes, k=number of lists
# Space: O(k) for heap
# Alt: divide-and-conquer merge pairs → same O(n log k)

follow_up_questions: Why is the index i needed as tiebreaker in the heap tuple?
Compare heap approach vs divide-and-conquer — same complexity, when would you use each?
What's the complexity of the naive approach (merge one list at a time)?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Min-heap of size k. Pop min, push its next. O(n log k), O(k). Alt: pair-wise divide-and-conquer.

algo_problems.title: Reverse Nodes in K-Group
thinking_questions: You need k nodes at a time. What do you do if fewer than k nodes remain?
Once you reverse a group of k, how do you connect it back to the rest of the list?
Can you recurse on the remaining list after processing each group?

hints.L1: Collect k nodes, reverse them, connect the tail to the recursively processed remainder. If fewer than k remain, leave them as-is.
hints.L2: Count k nodes ahead. If k available: reverse the group, connect tail to recurse(next group). Track the new head and old head (which becomes the tail).
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: check k nodes exist, reverse group, recurse on rest
def reverseKGroup(head, k):
    # Check if k nodes exist
    curr, count = head, 0
    while curr and count < k: curr = curr.next; count += 1
    if count < k: return head  # fewer than k — leave unchanged
    # Reverse k nodes
    prev, curr = None, head
    for _ in range(k):
        nxt = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev, curr = curr, nxt
    # head is now tail of reversed group; connect to next group
    head.next = reverseKGroup(curr, k)
    return prev  # prev is new head of reversed group
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(n/k) recursion depth

follow_up_questions: Why does head.next = reverseKGroup(curr, k) correctly connect the reversed group to the rest?
How do you convert this to an iterative solution? What would you need to track?
What's the recursion depth? When would you prefer iterative over recursive?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Recursion: check k exist, reverse group, head.next=recurse(rest). O(n), O(n/k) stack depth.

algo_problems.title: Invert Binary Tree
thinking_questions: To invert a tree, what do you need to do at each node?
After swapping the children, do you need to do anything else?
Can you think of the base case — what do you do with a null node?

hints.L1: At each node, swap the left and right children, then recursively invert both subtrees.
hints.L2: Recursion: swap root.left and root.right, then recurse on both. Base case: None returns None.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: swap children at each node, recurse both subtrees
def invertTree(root):
    if not root: return None
    root.left, root.right = root.right, root.left
    invertTree(root.left)
    invertTree(root.right)
    return root
# Time: O(n) — visit every node
# Space: O(h) — recursion stack, h=height (O(log n) balanced, O(n) worst)

follow_up_questions: Can you implement this iteratively using a queue (BFS) or stack (DFS)?
Does it matter whether you swap before or after recursing?
What's the space complexity for a balanced tree vs a completely skewed (degenerate) tree?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Swap children, recurse both. O(n), O(h). Can also be done iteratively with a queue.

algo_problems.title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
thinking_questions: The depth of a tree = 1 + max depth of its subtrees. Can you express that recursively?
What's the base case?
Can you solve this iteratively with BFS (level by level)?

hints.L1: Depth = 1 + max(depth(left), depth(right)). Think recursively.
hints.L2: Base case: None → 0. Recursive: 1 + max(maxDepth(left), maxDepth(right)).
hints.L3:
def maxDepth(root):
    if not root: return 0
    return 1 + max(maxDepth(root.left), maxDepth(root.right))
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(h)
# BFS alt: count levels using queue
# from collections import deque
# q=deque([root]); depth=0
# while q: depth+=1; for _ in range(len(q)): node=q.popleft(); add children
# return depth

follow_up_questions: Implement the BFS (level-by-level) iterative version.
What's the difference between height and depth of a node?
How would you find the MINIMUM depth? (LC 111 — what edge case matters?)
algo_problems.solution_notes: 1+max(depth(left),depth(right)). O(n), O(h). BFS alt counts levels.

algo_problems.title: Diameter of Binary Tree
thinking_questions: The diameter is the longest path — which might or might not pass through the root. At each node, what's the longest path through it?
The path through a node uses the height of its left subtree and right subtree. How do you track the maximum over all nodes?
Can you compute height and diameter simultaneously in one DFS?

hints.L1: The diameter is max over all nodes of (left_height + right_height). Compute it during a height DFS.
hints.L2: Recursive DFS returns height. At each node, update global max with left_height+right_height. Return 1+max(left,right) as height.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: diameter at each node = left_height + right_height; track global max
result = [0]  # mutable container for global max
def dfs(node):
    if not node: return 0
    left = dfs(node.left)
    right = dfs(node.right)
    result[0] = max(result[0], left + right)  # path through this node
    return 1 + max(left, right)  # return height
dfs(root)
return result[0]
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(h)

follow_up_questions: What if the tree is empty? What if it's a single node?
Why might the longest path NOT pass through the root?
How would you extend this to find the actual path, not just its length?
algo_problems.solution_notes: DFS returns height; at each node update diameter=left+right. O(n), O(h).

algo_problems.title: Balanced Binary Tree
thinking_questions: Definition: height-balanced = every node has left and right subtree heights differing by ≤1. Does this need to hold for every node or just the root?
Naive: compute height of every node separately — that's O(n) per node = O(n²). Can you compute balance and height simultaneously?
What should your DFS return to communicate both height and imbalance detection?

hints.L1: A tree is balanced only if EVERY node's subtrees differ by ≤1 in height. Check this in a single DFS by returning height or -1 for imbalanced.
hints.L2: DFS returns -1 if subtree is imbalanced, otherwise returns height. If either child returns -1, or |left-right|>1, return -1. Otherwise return 1+max(left,right).
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: return -1 as sentinel for imbalanced; otherwise return height
def dfs(node):
    if not node: return 0
    left = dfs(node.left)
    right = dfs(node.right)
    if left == -1 or right == -1: return -1  # subtree imbalanced
    if abs(left - right) > 1: return -1      # this node imbalanced
    return 1 + max(left, right)
return dfs(root) != -1
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(h)

follow_up_questions: Why return -1 as a sentinel rather than a boolean?
What's the complexity of the naive approach (separate height call per node)?
Could you solve this top-down instead of bottom-up? What's the difference in complexity?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Bottom-up DFS: return -1 for imbalance, height otherwise. O(n). -1 sentinel avoids separate imbalanced flag.

algo_problems.title: Same Tree
thinking_questions: Two trees are the same if their structures and values are identical. What are the base cases?
What are the four cases when comparing two nodes?
Can you solve this iteratively using a stack or queue?

hints.L1: Recursively compare: both null = same; one null = different; values differ = different; else recurse on both children.
hints.L2: Four cases: (1) both None → True. (2) one None → False. (3) values differ → False. (4) recurse left and right.

follow_up_questions: How does this differ from checking if two trees are structurally the same but values don't matter?
How would you check if a tree is symmetric? (LC 101)
Implement this iteratively using a stack.
algo_problems.solution_notes: Four base cases, recurse both subtrees. O(n), O(h).

algo_problems.title: Subtree of Another Tree
thinking_questions: How does this relate to the Same Tree problem?
At each node of the main tree, you could check if the subtree rooted there is the same as subRoot. What's the complexity?
Can you serialize both trees and use string matching for O(m+n)?

hints.L1: For each node in the main tree, check if the tree rooted there is identical to subRoot. Use your isSameTree function.
hints.L2: DFS: if root is None return False. If isSameTree(root, subRoot) return True. Else recurse on left and right.

follow_up_questions: What's the string serialization approach and its complexity?
How does the order of DFS matter — should you check isSameTree before or after recursing?
What if both trees are huge — is O(m·n) acceptable?
algo_problems.solution_notes: DFS on root; at each node call isSameTree(node, subRoot). O(m·n). String serialization gives O(m+n).

algo_problems.title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree
thinking_questions: The BST property tells you which direction to go. If both p and q are smaller than root, where is the LCA?
If p and q are on opposite sides of root (or one equals root), what is the LCA?
Can you solve this iteratively without recursion?

hints.L1: Use BST property: if both nodes are smaller than root, LCA is in left subtree. If both larger, right subtree. If they split (or one equals root), root is the LCA.
hints.L2: Traverse: if p.val < root.val and q.val < root.val → go left. If both > root.val → go right. Else → root is LCA.

follow_up_questions: How does this change for a general binary tree (not BST)? (LC 236)
What if the tree were a BST but not balanced — what's the worst case?
Is the iterative or recursive version preferred? Why?
algo_problems.solution_notes: BST: go left if both smaller, right if both larger, else current = LCA. O(log n) balanced, O(1) space iterative.

algo_problems.title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
thinking_questions: You need to process nodes level by level — what traversal strategy naturally works level by level?
How do you know which nodes belong to the current level vs the next?
How do you process exactly one level at a time from a queue?

hints.L1: BFS naturally processes level by level. Use a queue and process all nodes at the current level before moving to the next.
hints.L2: Queue initialized with root. For each level, process len(queue) nodes (that's the current level size), collect values, add children.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: BFS level-by-level; snapshot queue size at start of each level
from collections import deque
result = []
if not root: return result
q = deque([root])
while q:
    level = []
    for _ in range(len(q)):  # process exactly this level
        node = q.popleft()
        level.append(node.val)
        if node.left: q.append(node.left)
        if node.right: q.append(node.right)
    result.append(level)
return result
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(n)

follow_up_questions: Why does `for _ in range(len(q))` correctly isolate one level?
How would you do reverse level order traversal? (LC 107)
How would you do zigzag level order traversal? (LC 103)
algo_problems.solution_notes: BFS with queue. Snapshot len(q) at start of each level to process one level at a time. O(n).

algo_problems.title: Binary Tree Right Side View
thinking_questions: You want the last node at each level — how do you get that?
BFS gives you level by level — at each level, which node is the rightmost?
Can you solve this with DFS by visiting right before left and tracking depth?

hints.L1: BFS: at each level, the last node processed is the rightmost. Collect the last value of each level.
hints.L2: Level order BFS. For each level, after processing all nodes, record the last node's value.

follow_up_questions: How would you get the LEFT side view instead?
DFS approach: visit right child before left, track depth — explain how that captures the rightmost node.
What if you needed both left and right side views simultaneously?
algo_problems.solution_notes: BFS level order; last value per level is the right-side view. O(n). DFS alt: right-first DFS, first node seen per depth.

algo_problems.title: Count Good Nodes in Binary Tree
thinking_questions: A good node has no value greater than itself on the path from root. What do you need to track as you recurse down?
If you pass the current max value down the recursion, how do you determine if the current node is good?
What's the initial max value to pass to the root?

hints.L1: A node is 'good' if its value >= the max value seen on the path from root to it. Track the max as you recurse.
hints.L2: DFS with max_so_far parameter. Node is good if node.val >= max_so_far. Update max_so_far = max(max_so_far, node.val) before recursing.

follow_up_questions: What's the initial max_val passed to the root, and why?
How would you count 'bad' nodes (value less than max on path)?
Can you solve this iteratively with a stack storing (node, max_val) tuples?
algo_problems.solution_notes: DFS passing max_so_far. Node good if val >= max. Update max before recursing. O(n), O(h).

algo_problems.title: Validate Binary Search Tree
thinking_questions: Common mistake: only checking each node against its direct parent. Why doesn't that work? Give an example.
What are the actual bounds on each node's value given its position in the tree?
If you pass min and max bounds down the recursion, how do they update for left vs right children?

hints.L1: Checking only against parent isn't enough — a node in the right subtree must be greater than ALL ancestors, not just its parent. Pass valid bounds down the recursion.
hints.L2: DFS with (min_bound, max_bound). Root: (-inf, +inf). Left child: (min, node.val). Right child: (node.val, max). Node valid if min < node.val < max.

follow_up_questions: Give a concrete example where checking only against direct parent fails.
Can you solve this with inorder traversal? What property of BST inorder traversal is useful?
What if equal values were allowed on one side? How would you modify the bounds?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Pass (min, max) bounds down. Left updates max=node.val; right updates min=node.val. O(n), O(h). Alt: inorder traversal must be strictly increasing.

algo_problems.title: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
thinking_questions: Inorder traversal of a BST visits nodes in sorted order. How does that help?
Can you stop early once you've found the kth element?
Is recursive or iterative inorder more natural for early stopping?

hints.L1: Inorder traversal of a BST gives nodes in sorted order. The kth element visited is the kth smallest.
hints.L2: Iterative inorder with a stack. Push left, pop (visit), then go right. Stop after kth visit.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: BST inorder = sorted order; kth visited = kth smallest
stack, curr, count = [], root, 0
while stack or curr:
    while curr:  # go as left as possible
        stack.append(curr)
        curr = curr.left
    curr = stack.pop()  # visit
    count += 1
    if count == k: return curr.val
    curr = curr.right
# Time: O(h+k)  Space: O(h)

follow_up_questions: If the BST is frequently modified and you need kth smallest repeatedly, how would you optimize? (augment nodes with subtree size)
What's the difference in complexity between O(n) and O(h+k)? When does it matter?
How would you find the kth LARGEST element?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Iterative inorder traversal, stop at kth. O(h+k). BST inorder = sorted; kth visited = kth smallest.

algo_problems.title: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
thinking_questions: Preorder: root is always first. How does that help you find the root?
Once you know the root, how does the inorder array tell you which nodes are in the left vs right subtree?
After splitting, what do you recurse on?

hints.L1: Preorder's first element is always the root. Find that root in inorder to determine the left/right subtree sizes.
hints.L2: Root = preorder[0]. Find its index in inorder. Left subtree has inorder[:idx] elements, right has inorder[idx+1:]. Recurse on corresponding preorder slices.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: preorder[0]=root; inorder split defines left/right subtrees
if not preorder or not inorder: return None
root_val = preorder[0]
root = TreeNode(root_val)
mid = inorder.index(root_val)
root.left = buildTree(preorder[1:1+mid], inorder[:mid])
root.right = buildTree(preorder[1+mid:], inorder[mid+1:])
return root
# Time: O(n²) due to list slicing and .index()
# Optimize to O(n): HashMap for inorder index lookup + pass indices instead of slicing

follow_up_questions: How do you optimize from O(n²) to O(n) using a HashMap?
What if you had postorder and inorder instead? How does the approach change?
Why can you NOT construct a unique tree from preorder alone?
algo_problems.solution_notes: preorder[0]=root; find in inorder for left/right split; recurse. O(n²) naive, O(n) with HashMap for inorder indices.

algo_problems.title: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
thinking_questions: A path can start anywhere and end anywhere. At each node, what options do you have for the path that passes through it?
When you return a value from recursion, what does the parent need — the full path through you, or just the best single-side path?
Why do you need to take max(left, 0) and max(right, 0)?

hints.L1: At each node the best path through it = node.val + max(left_path, 0) + max(right_path, 0). But you can only extend one side to the parent.
hints.L2: DFS returns the best single-direction gain (node.val + max(one_child, 0)). At each node, update global max with node.val + max(left,0) + max(right,0).
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: local = both sides; return value = best single side to parent
result = [float('-inf')]
def dfs(node):
    if not node: return 0
    left = max(dfs(node.left), 0)   # ignore negative paths
    right = max(dfs(node.right), 0)
    # path through this node (cannot be extended up through both sides)
    result[0] = max(result[0], node.val + left + right)
    # return best single-direction extension to parent
    return node.val + max(left, right)
dfs(root)
return result[0]
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(h)

follow_up_questions: Why max(left, 0) and not just left? What does taking max with 0 represent?
Why can't you return node.val + left + right to the parent?
What if you needed to return the actual path, not just the sum?
algo_problems.solution_notes: DFS: update global max with both-sides sum; return best single-side gain to parent. max(child,0) ignores negative branches. O(n).

algo_problems.title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
thinking_questions: You need a format that captures both structure and values — including null positions. Which traversal works?
What do you need to include in the serialized string to reconstruct nulls (missing children)?
Preorder serialization with explicit null markers — how do you deserialize it?

hints.L1: Use preorder DFS with explicit null markers. The preorder format uniquely encodes structure when nulls are included.
hints.L2: Serialize: preorder DFS, join values with commas, use 'N' for null. Deserialize: split by comma, use an index pointer (or queue) to reconstruct.

follow_up_questions: Why do you need explicit null markers? What goes wrong without them?
How does the iterative deserialization using iter(vals) work — why does it naturally match the recursive structure?
How would you serialize using BFS (level order) instead?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Preorder DFS + 'N' null markers. Deserialize with iterator. O(n). BFS alternative uses level-order with explicit nulls.

algo_problems.title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
thinking_questions: A Trie stores words character by character. What does each node represent?
How do you mark the end of a word vs a prefix?
At each node you need up to 26 children — how do you store them?

hints.L1: Each node represents a character position. Store children (up to 26) and a flag marking if this node is a word end.
hints.L2: TrieNode with children dict (or array[26]) and is_end flag. Insert: walk/create nodes per char, set is_end at last. Search: walk nodes, check is_end. StartsWith: walk nodes, return True if all chars found.

follow_up_questions: When would you use an array[26] for children vs a HashMap? What are the tradeoffs?
How would you add a delete operation to the Trie?
How does a Trie compare to a HashSet for prefix queries?
algo_problems.solution_notes: TrieNode: children dict + is_end. Insert/search/startsWith all O(n). Key: is_end distinguishes 'app' from 'apple'.

algo_problems.title: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
thinking_questions: This is a Trie with wildcards. What does '.' match?
When you hit a '.' in the pattern, what do you have to do?
How do you handle '.' efficiently in the Trie traversal?

hints.L1: Build on Implement Trie. The '.' wildcard requires trying all possible children — use DFS/recursion at wildcard positions.
hints.L2: addWord: same as Trie insert. search: DFS on trie. At '.' try all non-null children recursively. At regular char, proceed normally.

follow_up_questions: What's the worst-case complexity for search with all '.' characters? Why?
How would you handle multi-character wildcards like '*'?
When would you prefer a simple list + regex over a Trie for this problem?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Trie + DFS for '.' wildcard. At '.', recurse into all children. Worst case O(26^n) for all-dot pattern.

algo_problems.title: Word Search II
thinking_questions: Word Search I finds one word. Here you have multiple words — what's inefficient about running Word Search I for each?
If you build a Trie of all words and search the board against it simultaneously, what do you gain?
How do you prevent revisiting cells during a DFS path?

hints.L1: Build a Trie of all words. DFS from every cell, navigating the Trie simultaneously — prune when the current path isn't a valid prefix.
