algo_problems.title: Interleaving String
thinking_questions: dp[i][j] = can s1[:i] and s2[:j] interleave to form s3[:i+j]. What are the transitions?
What does it mean to 'take from s1' vs 'take from s2'?
What is the base case dp[0][0]?

hints.L1: 2D DP: dp[i][j] = can s1[:i] and s2[:j] interleave to form s3[:i+j].
hints.L2: dp[i][j] = (dp[i-1][j] and s1[i-1]==s3[i+j-1]) OR (dp[i][j-1] and s2[j-1]==s3[i+j-1]). Use 1D rolling array.
hints.L3:
# Interleaving String — O(m*n) time, O(n) space
# KEY INSIGHT: dp[i][j] = can we form s3[:i+j] using s1[:i] and s2[:j]

def isInterleave(s1, s2, s3):
    m, n = len(s1), len(s2)
    if m + n != len(s3):
        return False

    dp = [False] * (n + 1)
    dp[0] = True  # empty strings interleave to form empty string

    # Fill first row (using only s2)
    for j in range(1, n + 1):
        dp[j] = dp[j-1] and s2[j-1] == s3[j-1]

    for i in range(1, m + 1):
        dp[0] = dp[0] and s1[i-1] == s3[i-1]  # first col (only s1)
        for j in range(1, n + 1):
            k = i + j - 1  # index in s3
            dp[j] = (dp[j] and s1[i-1] == s3[k]) or                     (dp[j-1] and s2[j-1] == s3[k])

    return dp[n]

follow_up_questions: Why is length check (m+n != len(s3)) an important early exit?
How do you debug the dp transitions mentally?
Could you solve this with BFS treating each state as (i, j)?
algo_problems.solution_notes: 2D DP compressed to 1D. dp[i][j] = can s1[:i] and s2[:j] form s3[:i+j]. Transitions: take from s1 or take from s2.

algo_problems.title: Longest Increasing Path in a Matrix
thinking_questions: From each cell, you can go to any of 4 neighbors if they're strictly greater. Can you DFS + memoize?
Why can you guarantee no cycles in the DFS? (strict increase prevents revisiting)
What does memo[i][j] represent?

hints.L1: DFS from every cell, but memoize: memo[i][j] = length of longest increasing path starting at (i,j).
hints.L2: DFS with memo: from each cell, try all 4 neighbors that are strictly larger. Cache results — no revisit needed because strictly increasing paths can't have cycles.

follow_up_questions: Why can't you have a cycle in the DFS (why is memoization safe)?
What's the topological sort approach using in-degree?
How would you reconstruct the actual path?
algo_problems.solution_notes: DFS + memo. No cycles because strictly increasing prevents revisiting. Try all 4 neighbors with val > current. O(m*n) time.

algo_problems.title: Distinct Subsequences
thinking_questions: dp[i][j] = number of ways t[:j] appears as a subsequence of s[:i]. What are the transitions?
When s[i]==t[j], how many ways are there? When they don't match?
What does dp[i][0] = 1 for all i mean?

hints.L1: 2D DP: count how many ways t can appear as a subsequence of s up to each position.
hints.L2: dp[i][j] = ways t[:j] appears in s[:i]. If s[i-1]==t[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + dp[i-1][j] (use or skip char). Else: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j].
hints.L3:
# Distinct Subsequences — O(m*n) time, O(n) space
# KEY INSIGHT: at each s[i], decide: use it to match t[j], or skip it

def numDistinct(s, t):
    m, n = len(s), len(t)
    # dp[j] = ways t[:j] appears in s so far
    dp = [0] * (n + 1)
    dp[0] = 1  # empty t is a subsequence of any s (1 way)

    for i in range(1, m + 1):
        # Process RIGHT to LEFT to avoid using s[i] multiple times
        for j in range(n, 0, -1):
            if s[i-1] == t[j-1]:
                dp[j] += dp[j-1]  # use s[i] to match t[j], OR don't use it (dp[j] already included)

    return dp[n]
# WHY right to left: same pattern as 0/1 knapsack

follow_up_questions: Why does the inner loop go right to left?
What's the relationship between this and LCS?
How does dp[j] += dp[j-1] handle both 'use' and 'skip' cases?
algo_problems.solution_notes: dp[j] = ways t[:j] is subsequence of s so far. Match: dp[j] += dp[j-1]. Iterate j right-to-left (0/1 knapsack pattern).

algo_problems.title: Edit Distance
thinking_questions: dp[i][j] = min edits to convert word1[:i] to word2[:j]. What are the 3 operations?
If word1[i]==word2[j], no edit needed — you can take dp[i-1][j-1]. What if they differ?
What do the base cases dp[i][0]=i and dp[0][j]=j represent?

hints.L1: At each position, try all 3 operations (insert, delete, replace) and take the minimum plus 1.
hints.L2: dp[i][j]: if chars match = dp[i-1][j-1]. Else = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j-1] replace, dp[i-1][j] delete, dp[i][j-1] insert). Base: dp[i][0]=i, dp[0][j]=j.
hints.L3:
# Edit Distance — O(m*n) time, O(n) space
# KEY INSIGHT: dp[i][j] = min edits to convert word1[:i] → word2[:j]
# 3 operations: replace (diagonal), delete from w1 (above), insert into w1 (left)

def minDistance(word1, word2):
    m, n = len(word1), len(word2)
    # dp[j] represents current row
    dp = list(range(n + 1))  # base case: dp[0][j] = j

    for i in range(1, m + 1):
        prev = dp[0]          # save dp[i-1][j-1]
        dp[0] = i             # base case: dp[i][0] = i

        for j in range(1, n + 1):
            temp = dp[j]      # save before overwrite (= dp[i-1][j])
            if word1[i-1] == word2[j-1]:
                dp[j] = prev  # no edit needed: take diagonal
            else:
                dp[j] = 1 + min(prev,    # replace
                                dp[j],   # delete from word1
                                dp[j-1]) # insert into word1
            prev = temp       # move diagonal forward

    return dp[n]

follow_up_questions: What does each of the 3 operations correspond to in the dp table?
How would you reconstruct the actual sequence of edits?
What's the relationship between Edit Distance and LCS?
algo_problems.solution_notes: dp[i][j]: match=dp[i-1][j-1], else 1+min(replace diagonal, delete above, insert left). Base: dp[i][0]=i, dp[0][j]=j.

algo_problems.title: Burst Balloons
thinking_questions: Why is thinking about the LAST balloon burst in a range easier than the first?
If balloon k is the last burst in range [l, r], what coins do you get from bursting k?
Why do you add sentinel 1s at both ends of the array?

hints.L1: Think about which balloon to burst LAST in each interval (not first) — it simplifies the dependency.
hints.L2: Add 1s at both ends. dp[l][r] = max coins from bursting all balloons in (l,r) exclusive. For each k as last to burst: dp[l][r] = max(dp[l][k] + nums[l]*nums[k]*nums[r] + dp[k][r]).
hints.L3:
# Burst Balloons — O(n^3) time, O(n^2) space
# KEY INSIGHT: think about LAST balloon burst in interval, not first
# Pad with 1s: nums = [1] + nums + [1]

def maxCoins(nums):
    nums = [1] + nums + [1]
    n = len(nums)
    dp = [[0] * n for _ in range(n)]

    # l = interval length
    for length in range(2, n):
        for l in range(0, n - length):
            r = l + length
            for k in range(l + 1, r):  # k is LAST burst in (l, r)
                # coins: left boundary * k * right boundary (both still exist when k bursts)
                coins = nums[l] * nums[k] * nums[r]
                dp[l][r] = max(dp[l][r], dp[l][k] + coins + dp[k][r])

    return dp[0][n-1]
# WHY last burst: when k is last, nums[l] and nums[r] are still present as neighbors

follow_up_questions: Why does thinking about the LAST balloon rather than first work?
What does the open interval (l, r) exclusive mean in this dp?
How do the sentinel 1s simplify boundary handling?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Interval DP. Pad [1]+nums+[1]. dp[l][r]=max coins bursting all in open interval (l,r). k = last burst: coins = nums[l]*nums[k]*nums[r].

algo_problems.title: Regular Expression Matching
thinking_questions: dp[i][j] = does s[:i] match p[:j]? How do you handle '.' vs '*'?
When p[j-1] == '*': two cases — use zero of preceding (dp[i][j-2]) or use one more (dp[i-1][j] if chars match).
What is the base case dp[0][0]? What about dp[0][j] when pattern has 'a*b*c*'?

hints.L1: 2D DP: dp[i][j] = does s[:i] match p[:j]. Handle '.' as wildcard single char, '*' as zero-or-more of preceding char.
hints.L2: If p[j-1]=='*': dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-2] (zero uses) OR (if s[i-1] matches p[j-2]): dp[i-1][j] (one more use). If p[j-1]=='.' or exact match: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1].

follow_up_questions: How does handling '*' with dp[i][j-2] cover the zero-occurrences case?
Why do you need to initialize dp[0][j] for patterns like 'a*b*'?
How would you extend this to support '+' (one or more)?
algo_problems.solution_notes: 2D DP. '*' = zero uses (dp[i][j-2]) or more uses (dp[i-1][j] if match). '.' matches any. Init dp[0][j] for x*y* patterns.

algo_problems.title: Maximum Subarray
thinking_questions: If your running sum becomes negative, should you keep it or reset?
At each step, should you extend the current subarray or start a new one?
How does this greedy choice guarantee the global maximum?

hints.L1: If your running sum becomes negative, it can only hurt future sums — reset it to zero.
hints.L2: Kadane's algorithm: curSum = max(num, curSum + num). Track global max. A negative running sum is never worth keeping.
hints.L3:
# Maximum Subarray (Kadane's) — O(n) time, O(1) space
# KEY INSIGHT: if running sum is negative, reset — it hurts any future subarray

def maxSubArray(nums):
    cur_sum = 0
    max_sum = nums[0]  # handle all-negative arrays

    for num in nums:
        if cur_sum < 0:
            cur_sum = 0  # reset: negative prefix never helps
        cur_sum += num
        max_sum = max(max_sum, cur_sum)

    return max_sum
# EQUIVALENT: cur_sum = max(num, cur_sum + num)
# PITFALL: init max_sum to nums[0], not 0 (all negatives)

follow_up_questions: What if you needed to return the actual subarray (not just sum)?
What if the array wraps around (maximum circular subarray)?
Can you solve this with divide and conquer?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Kadane's: reset cur_sum to 0 when negative. cur_sum = max(num, cur_sum + num). Init max_sum to nums[0] not 0.

algo_problems.title: Jump Game
thinking_questions: Track the farthest index you can reach. At each position, can you update it?
What happens if your current position exceeds the farthest reachable index?
Why is a greedy approach (tracking max reach) sufficient?

hints.L1: Track the farthest index you can reach. If you ever go past it, you're stuck.
hints.L2: Greedy: maxReach = 0. For each i, if i > maxReach: return False. Else: maxReach = max(maxReach, i + nums[i]).
hints.L3:
# Jump Game — O(n) time, O(1) space
# KEY INSIGHT: track max reachable index greedily

def canJump(nums):
    max_reach = 0

    for i in range(len(nums)):
        if i > max_reach:
            return False  # stuck: can't reach this index

        max_reach = max(max_reach, i + nums[i])

    return True
# WHY greedy: at each reachable position, extend max_reach as far as possible
# If max_reach >= n-1 at any point, we're done

follow_up_questions: What's the minimum number of jumps to reach the end? (Jump Game II)
How would you return the actual jump path?
What if values can be negative?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Track max_reach. If i > max_reach: unreachable. Update max_reach = max(max_reach, i + nums[i]). O(n) time.

algo_problems.title: Jump Game II
thinking_questions: Think of this like BFS levels: all positions reachable in k jumps form one 'level'. What's the farthest you can reach from this level?
When do you increment the jump count?
What are farthest (max reach in current level) and curEnd (boundary of current level)?

hints.L1: Think of jumps as BFS levels. At each level, extend as far as possible before taking the next jump.
hints.L2: Track curEnd (current jump boundary) and farthest (max reachable from current level). When i reaches curEnd, increment jumps and set curEnd = farthest.
hints.L3:
# Jump Game II — O(n) time, O(1) space
# KEY INSIGHT: BFS level approach — each jump is one level
# Greedily extend farthest reach, jump when hitting level boundary

def jump(nums):
    jumps = 0
    cur_end = 0   # rightmost index in current level
    farthest = 0  # farthest we can reach from current level

    for i in range(len(nums) - 1):  # don't need to jump from last
        farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i])

        if i == cur_end:       # reached end of current level
            jumps += 1
            cur_end = farthest  # next level ends at farthest

    return jumps
# WHY stop at n-2: if we're at last index, no more jumps needed

follow_up_questions: Why do you loop to len(nums)-1 and not len(nums)?
How does this relate to BFS on a graph?
What if you wanted the actual jump path?
algo_problems.solution_notes: BFS levels. farthest=max reach, curEnd=level boundary. When i==curEnd: jump++, curEnd=farthest. Loop to n-2.

algo_problems.title: Gas Station
thinking_questions: If total gas < total cost, is a solution possible at all?
If you run out at station j starting from station i, can any station between i and j be a valid start?
What do you reset when you find you can't complete the circuit?

hints.L1: If total gas < total cost, no solution exists. Otherwise, the answer is always the station after where you last ran out.
hints.L2: Track cumulative tank. If tank < 0, reset start to next station and reset tank to 0. The valid start is where you reset last. Verify with total sum check.
hints.L3:
# Gas Station — O(n) time, O(1) space
# KEY INSIGHT: if total gas >= total cost, a solution exists
# The start is always right after where you last ran out

def canCompleteCircuit(gas, cost):
    total = 0      # total gas - total cost
    tank = 0       # current tank
    start = 0      # candidate starting station

    for i in range(len(gas)):
        net = gas[i] - cost[i]
        tank += net
        total += net

        if tank < 0:          # can't reach next station from current start
            start = i + 1     # try starting from next station
            tank = 0          # reset tank

    return start if total >= 0 else -1
# WHY: if you fail at i starting from s, then s+1..i all fail too (they'd start with less)

follow_up_questions: Why is the candidate start always right after the last failure?
Why does the total sum check guarantee uniqueness of the answer?
What if multiple solutions exist?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Track tank + total. When tank<0: reset start=i+1, tank=0. Return start if total>=0 else -1. O(n).

algo_problems.title: Hand of Straights
thinking_questions: What's the smallest card remaining? It must start a group. Why?
Use a sorted frequency map. For each group starting at smallest card, consume groupSize consecutive cards.
What happens if any consecutive card count goes to 0 before the group is complete?

hints.L1: The smallest remaining card must always start a new group. Greedily form groups from the minimum.
hints.L2: Use an ordered counter (sorted map). Start each group from the minimum key. Decrement groupSize consecutive keys. If any goes negative, return False.

follow_up_questions: Is this the same as 'Divide Array in Sets of K Consecutive Numbers'?
Why must the smallest card always start a group?
What's the time complexity with sorted dict vs heap?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Greedy: smallest always starts a group. Counter + min-heap. For each group, consume groupSize consecutive cards.

algo_problems.title: Merge Triplets to Form Target Triplet
thinking_questions: Merging takes the max of each position. When is a triplet 'bad' to include?
A triplet is bad if any value exceeds the target at that position. Why?
Can you check which target positions are achievable by scanning valid triplets?

hints.L1: Ignore triplets that exceed target in any position — they can never help. Check if remaining triplets can form the target.
hints.L2: Filter: skip triplets[i] if any triplets[i][j] > target[j]. Merge valid triplets by taking max at each position. Check if result == target.
hints.L3:
# Merge Triplets to Form Target — O(n) time, O(1) space
# KEY INSIGHT: skip any triplet exceeding target in any position (can't undo max)

def mergeTriplets(triplets, target):
    result = [0, 0, 0]

    for t in triplets:
        # Skip triplet if it exceeds target in any position
        if t[0] > target[0] or t[1] > target[1] or t[2] > target[2]:
            continue

        # Merge by taking max (this is what the merge operation does)
        result[0] = max(result[0], t[0])
        result[1] = max(result[1], t[1])
        result[2] = max(result[2], t[2])

    return result == target

follow_up_questions: Why can't you include a triplet that exceeds target[i] in position i?
Is the order of merging relevant?
What if you had n-tuples instead of triplets?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Filter triplets exceeding target in any position. Merge valid ones by max. Check == target. O(n).

algo_problems.title: Partition Labels
thinking_questions: For a character to be in one partition, all its occurrences must be in that partition. What does this mean for the partition boundary?
Precompute the last occurrence of each character. How does this help set partition boundaries?
How do you extend the current partition's end when you encounter a character?

hints.L1: The end of each partition is determined by the last occurrence of any character seen so far.
hints.L2: Precompute last[c] = last index of char c. Scan left-to-right: extend end = max(end, last[s[i]]). When i == end, partition boundary found.
hints.L3:
# Partition Labels — O(n) time, O(1) space (O(26) for last array)
# KEY INSIGHT: partition boundary = last occurrence of any seen character

def partitionLabels(s):
    last = {c: i for i, c in enumerate(s)}  # last occurrence of each char
    result = []
    start = end = 0

    for i, c in enumerate(s):
        end = max(end, last[c])  # extend end to last occurrence of c

        if i == end:             # reached the partition boundary
            result.append(end - start + 1)
            start = end + 1

    return result

follow_up_questions: What if a character can span multiple partitions?
How does this relate to the Merge Intervals problem?
What's the minimum number of partitions if you could reassign characters?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Precompute last occurrence. Scan: extend end=max(end,last[c]). When i==end: partition done. O(n) time, O(1) space.

algo_problems.title: Valid Parenthesis String
thinking_questions: Instead of tracking one count, track a range [min, max] of possible open parentheses.
What does '*' do to the range? What does '(' and ')' do?
When is the range invalid (impossible to balance)? When is it valid at the end?

hints.L1: Track a range of possible open paren counts instead of one specific count.
hints.L2: lo = min possible opens, hi = max possible opens. '(': lo++,hi++. ')': lo--,hi--. '*': lo--,hi++. If hi<0: invalid. Clamp lo>=0. End: lo==0.
hints.L3:
# Valid Parenthesis String — O(n) time, O(1) space
# KEY INSIGHT: track [lo, hi] range of possible open paren counts
# '*' can be '(' (hi++), ')' (lo--), or '' (neither)

def checkValidString(s):
    lo = hi = 0  # [min, max] possible open parens

    for c in s:
        if c == '(':
            lo += 1; hi += 1
        elif c == ')':
            lo -= 1; hi -= 1
        else:  # '*'
            lo -= 1  # '*' as ')'
            hi += 1  # '*' as '('

        if hi < 0:
            return False  # too many closing parens even with all '*' as '('
        lo = max(lo, 0)   # clamp: open count can't be negative

    return lo == 0  # min possible opens = 0 means valid matching is possible

follow_up_questions: Why clamp lo to 0 instead of allowing it to go negative?
What does hi < 0 mean physically?
How would you solve this with a stack instead?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Range [lo,hi] of possible open counts. ')' narrows both, '(' widens both, '*' widens range. hi<0: fail. End: lo==0.

algo_problems.title: Insert Interval
thinking_questions: There are 3 cases for each existing interval relative to the new one: before, overlapping, after.
When do two intervals overlap? When does an existing interval come entirely before the new one?
How do you merge overlapping intervals as you scan?

hints.L1: Process intervals in three phases: before newInterval, overlapping with newInterval, after newInterval.
hints.L2: Before: existing[1] < new[0] → add as-is. Overlapping: new[0] <= existing[1] and existing[0] <= new[1] → merge (take min start, max end). After: existing[0] > new[1] → add rest.
hints.L3:
# Insert Interval — O(n) time, O(n) space
# 3 phases: before new interval, overlapping, after

def insert(intervals, newInterval):
    result = []
    i = 0
    n = len(intervals)

    # Phase 1: add all intervals that end before new starts
    while i < n and intervals[i][1] < newInterval[0]:
        result.append(intervals[i])
        i += 1

    # Phase 2: merge all overlapping intervals
    while i < n and intervals[i][0] <= newInterval[1]:
        newInterval[0] = min(newInterval[0], intervals[i][0])
        newInterval[1] = max(newInterval[1], intervals[i][1])
        i += 1
    result.append(newInterval)

    # Phase 3: add remaining intervals
    while i < n:
        result.append(intervals[i])
        i += 1

    return result

follow_up_questions: What if intervals are not pre-sorted?
How does this relate to Merge Intervals?
What are the exact overlap conditions?
algo_problems.solution_notes: 3-phase linear scan. Phase 1: end<newStart (add). Phase 2: start<=newEnd (merge). Phase 3: add rest. O(n).

algo_problems.title: Merge Intervals
thinking_questions: If you sort by start time, when can two adjacent intervals overlap?
How do you merge into the previous interval vs starting a new one?
What does 'merged[-1][1] >= curr[0]' tell you?

hints.L1: Sort by start time. Then scan: if current start <= last merged end, extend the merge. Otherwise start a new interval.
hints.L2: Sort by start. For each interval: if result is empty or result[-1][1] < curr[0]: append. Else: result[-1][1] = max(result[-1][1], curr[1]).
hints.L3:
# Merge Intervals — O(n log n) time, O(n) space
# KEY INSIGHT: after sorting by start, overlapping intervals are adjacent

def merge(intervals):
    intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
    result = [intervals[0]]

    for start, end in intervals[1:]:
        if start <= result[-1][1]:        # overlaps with last merged
            result[-1][1] = max(result[-1][1], end)  # extend end
        else:
            result.append([start, end])   # no overlap: new interval

    return result
# PITFALL: use max(result[-1][1], end) not just end (nested intervals)

follow_up_questions: Why do you need max() when merging ends, not just take curr[1]?
How would you handle an interval being completely contained?
What's the difference between this and Insert Interval?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Sort by start. Scan: overlap (start <= last end) → extend last end with max. No overlap → append new. O(n log n).

algo_problems.title: Non-overlapping Intervals
thinking_questions: To minimize removals, think about maximizing the intervals you KEEP.
Which interval should you keep when two overlap? The one that ends earlier — why?
This is the classic 'activity selection' greedy problem.

hints.L1: Greedy: when intervals overlap, remove the one that ends later (keep the one ending sooner to leave room for more).
hints.L2: Sort by end time. Track prevEnd. If curr[0] < prevEnd: conflict → remove current (count++). Else: update prevEnd = curr[1].
hints.L3:
# Non-overlapping Intervals — O(n log n) time, O(1) space
# KEY INSIGHT: greedy activity selection — keep interval ending earliest

def eraseOverlapIntervals(intervals):
    intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])  # sort by END time
    removals = 0
    prev_end = float('-inf')

    for start, end in intervals:
        if start >= prev_end:
            prev_end = end   # compatible: keep this interval
        else:
            removals += 1    # overlap: remove (current already has later end since sorted)

    return removals
# WHY sort by end: keeping earliest-ending interval is greedy optimal
# This is equivalent to finding max non-overlapping intervals

follow_up_questions: Why sort by end time rather than start time?
How is this the same as finding the maximum non-overlapping set?
What's the activity selection problem and how does it relate?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Sort by end. Greedy: keep earliest-ending non-conflicting interval. Count removals. O(n log n) sort.

algo_problems.title: Meeting Rooms
thinking_questions: If you sort by start time, when would two consecutive meetings conflict?
What does intervals[i][0] < intervals[i-1][1] mean?
Is there any case where non-adjacent intervals can overlap but adjacent ones don't?

hints.L1: Sort by start time. If any meeting starts before the previous one ends, you can't attend all.
hints.L2: Sort by start. Check if any intervals[i][0] < intervals[i-1][1]. If so, return False.
hints.L3:
# Meeting Rooms — O(n log n) time, O(1) space
# KEY INSIGHT: after sorting by start, overlaps only possible between adjacent intervals

def canAttendMeetings(intervals):
    intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])

    for i in range(1, len(intervals)):
        if intervals[i][0] < intervals[i-1][1]:  # new starts before prev ends
            return False

    return True

follow_up_questions: What if you want the minimum number of rooms needed? (Meeting Rooms II)
Why does sorting by start guarantee we only check adjacent pairs?
Is [1,5] and [5,10] overlapping or not?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Sort by start. Overlap iff intervals[i][0] < intervals[i-1][1]. O(n log n). Note: touching endpoints (equal) is NOT overlap.

algo_problems.title: Meeting Rooms II
thinking_questions: When can a new meeting reuse an existing room?
Use a min-heap of end times. What does the top of the heap represent?
Can you also solve this with a sorted start/end event sweep?

hints.L1: Use a min-heap of end times. If new meeting starts after earliest ending, reuse that room. Otherwise, open a new room.
hints.L2: Sort by start. Min-heap of end times. For each meeting: if heap[0] <= start: pop (room freed). Push current end. Answer = heap size at end.

follow_up_questions: What does the heap size represent at the end?
How does the sweep line approach work?
How is this different from Meeting Rooms I?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Sort by start. Min-heap of end times. If earliest end <= new start: reuse (heapreplace). Else: new room (heappush). Size = answer.

algo_problems.title: Minimum Interval to Include Each Query
thinking_questions: Sort queries offline (with original indices). Sort intervals by start.
As you sweep queries left to right, which intervals are 'active'?
Use a min-heap of (size, end) for active intervals. How do you remove expired ones?

hints.L1: Sort queries offline. Use a min-heap of active intervals (by size). Add intervals as their start <= query, remove intervals whose end < query.
hints.L2: Sort intervals by start, queries by value (with idx). Sweep: add intervals with start <= q to heap. Remove heap top while end < q. Answer = heap[0][0] or -1.
hints.L3:
# Minimum Interval to Include Query — O((n+q) log n)
# KEY INSIGHT: offline queries + sweep + min-heap of active interval sizes

import heapq

def minInterval(intervals, queries):
    intervals.sort()
    sorted_queries = sorted(enumerate(queries), key=lambda x: x[1])
    result = [-1] * len(queries)

    heap = []  # (size, end)
    i = 0      # pointer into sorted intervals

    for idx, q in sorted_queries:
        # Add all intervals starting at or before q
        while i < len(intervals) and intervals[i][0] <= q:
            start, end = intervals[i]
            heapq.heappush(heap, (end - start + 1, end))
            i += 1

        # Remove intervals that have already ended
        while heap and heap[0][1] < q:
            heapq.heappop(heap)

        if heap:
            result[idx] = heap[0][0]  # smallest active interval size

    return result

follow_up_questions: Why sort queries offline instead of online?
What does removing intervals with end < q guarantee?
How would you handle duplicate queries efficiently?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Sort intervals by start, queries by value offline. Sweep: add intervals with start<=q to min-heap by size, pop expired ends. O((n+q)logn).

algo_problems.title: Rotate Image
thinking_questions: Can you decompose a 90-degree clockwise rotation into two simpler operations?
What happens if you transpose the matrix (flip along diagonal) then reverse each row?
What does transpose do to element (i,j)? What does reversing each row do?

hints.L1: A 90-degree clockwise rotation = transpose + reverse each row.
hints.L2: Step 1: Transpose (swap matrix[i][j] and matrix[j][i] for i<j). Step 2: Reverse each row. Both O(n^2) in-place.
hints.L3:
# Rotate Image 90° clockwise — O(n^2) time, O(1) space
# KEY INSIGHT: clockwise 90° = transpose + reverse each row
# Counterclockwise 90° = transpose + reverse each column

def rotate(matrix):
    n = len(matrix)

    # Step 1: Transpose (reflect across main diagonal)
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i + 1, n):  # only upper triangle
            matrix[i][j], matrix[j][i] = matrix[j][i], matrix[i][j]

    # Step 2: Reverse each row
    for row in matrix:
        row.reverse()

# VERIFY: element at (r,c) → clockwise → (c, n-1-r)
# Transpose: (r,c)→(c,r). Reverse row: (c,r)→(c,n-1-r) ✓

follow_up_questions: How would you rotate 90° counterclockwise?
What's the mathematical transformation for each (row, col)?
How would you rotate 180°?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Clockwise = transpose + reverse rows. Counterclockwise = transpose + reverse columns. Both O(n^2) time, O(1) space.

algo_problems.title: Spiral Matrix
thinking_questions: How do you keep track of which cells you've already visited without extra space?
Can you use four boundary variables (top, bottom, left, right) to shrink the traversal area?
In what order do you traverse each layer: right, down, left, up?

hints.L1: Use four boundaries (top, bottom, left, right) and shrink them after traversing each edge.
hints.L2: Go right (top row), shrink top. Go down (right col), shrink right. Go left (bottom row), shrink bottom. Go up (left col), shrink left. Repeat while top<=bottom and left<=right.

follow_up_questions: Why do you need guards (top<=bottom and left<=right) for the left and up passes?
How would you generate a spiral matrix (Spiral Matrix II)?
What's the time complexity — is it O(m*n)?

algo_problems.title: Set Matrix Zeroes
thinking_questions: If you zero cells immediately, you'll incorrectly mark more rows/cols. How do you separate the marking and zeroing phases?
Can you use the first row and first column as markers? What special case arises for (0,0)?
Why do you process the matrix body before the first row/col?

hints.L1: First scan to mark which rows/cols need zeroing, then zero them — don't do it in one pass.
hints.L2: O(1) space: use first row and first column as markers. Special flag for (0,0) ambiguity. Process body first, then first row/col last.

follow_up_questions: Why process body before first row/col?
Why is there ambiguity at (0,0)? What does it represent?
What's the O(m+n) space solution and why is O(1) harder?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Use row 0 and col 0 as markers. Save first_row_zero and first_col_zero flags upfront. Zero body, then fix first row/col last.

algo_problems.title: Happy Number
thinking_questions: The sequence of digit-sum-of-squares either reaches 1 or cycles. How do you detect the cycle?
Can you use Floyd's cycle detection (slow/fast pointers) on this sequence?
What function maps n to its next value in the sequence?

hints.L1: The process either reaches 1 (happy) or enters a cycle. Detect the cycle with a set or Floyd's algorithm.
hints.L2: Floyd's cycle detection: slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps. If slow == fast and equals 1: happy. If they meet otherwise: cycle (not happy).
hints.L3:
# Happy Number — O(log n) time, O(1) space
# KEY INSIGHT: Floyd's cycle detection on digit-square sequence

def isHappy(n):
    def digit_square_sum(num):
        total = 0
        while num:
            digit = num % 10
            total += digit * digit
            num //= 10
        return total

    slow = n
    fast = digit_square_sum(n)

    while fast != 1 and slow != fast:
        slow = digit_square_sum(slow)
        fast = digit_square_sum(digit_square_sum(fast))

    return fast == 1
# SIMPLE ALTERNATIVE: use a set, detect if we've seen a number before
# Not happy numbers always reach 4 in their cycle

follow_up_questions: Why do non-happy numbers always form a cycle?
What's the O(1) space Floyd's approach vs O(log n) set approach?
What number do all non-happy sequences eventually reach?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Floyd's cycle detection. slow/fast on digit-square-sum function. Returns fast==1. Simpler: set-based O(log n) space.

algo_problems.title: Plus One
thinking_questions: Process from rightmost digit. What happens when you add 1 to 9?
When do you need to prepend a new digit?
What's the special case for all 9s (e.g., [9,9,9])?

hints.L1: Process digits from right to left. If digit < 9: increment and return. If 9: set to 0 and carry.
hints.L2: Iterate right to left. For each digit: if < 9, increment and return. Else, set to 0 (carry continues). If all were 9s: prepend 1.

follow_up_questions: How would you implement 'Plus K' for arbitrary k?
What if the input could have leading zeros?
How does this compare to big integer addition?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Right-to-left carry propagation. Early return if digit<9. Set to 0 if 9. Prepend 1 if all were 9s. O(n).

algo_problems.title: Pow(x, n)
thinking_questions: If you know x^(n/2), how do you get x^n in O(1)?
How do you handle negative exponents?
Iterative vs recursive: which avoids stack overflow for large n?

hints.L1: Use fast exponentiation: x^n = (x^(n//2))^2. Halve the problem each time for O(log n).
hints.L2: Fast power: if n is odd, multiply by x once. If n is even, just square. Handle n<0 as 1/x^(-n). Iterative avoids recursion overhead.
hints.L3:
# Pow(x, n) — O(log n) time, O(1) space (iterative)
# KEY INSIGHT: binary exponentiation — halve n each step

def myPow(x, n):
    if n < 0:
        x = 1 / x
        n = -n

    result = 1.0

    while n > 0:
        if n % 2 == 1:     # odd exponent: multiply result by current x
            result *= x
        x *= x             # square x
        n //= 2            # halve n

    return result
# TRACE: x^13 = x^8 * x^4 * x^1 (bits of 13 = 1101)
# Each bit of n determines whether to multiply result by current power

follow_up_questions: Why does binary exponentiation work — what's the invariant?
How does this relate to fast matrix exponentiation for Fibonacci?
How would you handle integer overflow in n?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Binary exponentiation. Odd n: result *= x. Square x, halve n. Handle n<0 with x=1/x. O(log n) iterative.

algo_problems.title: Multiply Strings
thinking_questions: How does grade-school multiplication work digit by digit?
If num1[i] * num2[j] contributes to position i+j and i+j+1 in the result, how do you track carries?
What's the maximum number of digits in the product of m-digit and n-digit numbers?

hints.L1: Simulate grade-school multiplication: each digit pair (i,j) contributes to positions i+j and i+j+1 in the result array.
hints.L2: Result array of size m+n. For each i,j: pos1=i+j, pos2=i+j+1. product = (num1[i]*num2[j]) + result[pos2]. result[pos2]=product%10, result[pos1]+=product//10.
hints.L3:
# Multiply Strings — O(m*n) time, O(m+n) space
# KEY INSIGHT: digit at num1[i] * num2[j] goes to positions [i+j, i+j+1]

def multiply(num1, num2):
    if num1 == "0" or num2 == "0":
        return "0"

    m, n = len(num1), len(num2)
    pos = [0] * (m + n)

    for i in range(m - 1, -1, -1):
        for j in range(n - 1, -1, -1):
            mul = (ord(num1[i]) - ord('0')) * (ord(num2[j]) - ord('0'))
            p1, p2 = i + j, i + j + 1
            total = mul + pos[p2]

            pos[p2] = total % 10
            pos[p1] += total // 10  # carry

    result = ''.join(str(d) for d in pos)
    return result.lstrip('0') or '0'

follow_up_questions: Why is the result array size m+n (not m+n-1)?
Why process digits from right to left?
How would you implement big integer addition?
algo_problems.solution_notes: pos[i+j] carries, pos[i+j+1] units. Process right-to-left. Accumulate carries. Strip leading zeros. O(m*n).

algo_problems.title: Detect Squares
thinking_questions: To form an axis-aligned square with query point Q, what are the constraints on the diagonal point?
Fix two points that share the same x-coordinate as the query (vertical edge). How do you find the other two?
How do you count efficiently using a point frequency map?

hints.L1: For each point sharing the same x-coordinate as query, check if a valid square can be completed using the count map.
hints.L2: For query point (px, py): enumerate all points (px, y) in points_on_x[px]. Width = |y - py|. Check both (px±width, y) and (px±width, py) exist. Multiply counts.
hints.L3:
# Detect Squares — O(n) add, O(sqrt(n)) count query
# KEY INSIGHT: fix vertical edge (same x as query), find horizontal width

from collections import defaultdict

class DetectSquares:
    def __init__(self):
        self.pt_count = defaultdict(int)   # (x,y) -> count
        self.x_to_ys = defaultdict(set)    # x -> set of y values

    def add(self, point):
        x, y = point
        self.pt_count[(x, y)] += 1
        self.x_to_ys[x].add(y)

    def count(self, point):
        px, py = point
        result = 0

        # Fix same-x points to get vertical edge
        for y in self.x_to_ys[px]:
            if y == py:
                continue
            width = abs(y - py)  # side length of square

            # Try both directions for the square (left and right)
            for dx in [width, -width]:
                # Other two corners: (px+dx, py) and (px+dx, y)
                result += (self.pt_count[(px, y)] *
                           self.pt_count[(px + dx, py)] *
                           self.pt_count[(px + dx, y)])

        return result

follow_up_questions: Why enumerate y values for the same x as the query point?
Why multiply three counts instead of checking existence only?
What if squares don't need to be axis-aligned?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Fix vertical edge on same x as query. Width=|y-py|. Try both ±width. Multiply counts of 3 other corners. O(sqrt(n)) per query.

algo_problems.title: Single Number
thinking_questions: What does XOR do to two identical numbers? What about a number XORed with 0?
If you XOR all numbers together, what happens to the pairs?
Why is XOR the perfect operation here?

hints.L1: XOR: a ^ a = 0, a ^ 0 = a. XOR all numbers — pairs cancel to 0, leaving the single number.
hints.L2: result = 0. For each num: result ^= num. All paired numbers cancel. Result is the single.
hints.L3:
# Single Number — O(n) time, O(1) space
# KEY INSIGHT: XOR is commutative & associative. a^a=0, a^0=a
# All pairs cancel, leaving the single number

def singleNumber(nums):
    result = 0
    for num in nums:
        result ^= num
    return result

# WHY: [4,1,2,1,2] → 4^1^2^1^2 = 4^(1^1)^(2^2) = 4^0^0 = 4
# PITFALL: this only works when all others appear EXACTLY twice

follow_up_questions: What if numbers appear 3 times instead of 2? (Single Number II)
What if two numbers appear once and rest appear twice? (Single Number III)
Why does order not matter for XOR?
algo_problems.solution_notes: XOR all numbers. Pairs cancel (a^a=0). Single remains. O(n) time, O(1) space. Works only for 'all others appear exactly twice'.

algo_problems.title: Number of 1 Bits
algo_problems.optimal_time: O(k) where k=number of set bits
thinking_questions: What does n & (n-1) do? How does it relate to the rightmost set bit?
How many times can you apply n = n & (n-1) before n becomes 0?
What's the naive O(32) approach, and why is n & (n-1) more elegant?

hints.L1: n & (n-1) clears the rightmost set bit. Count how many times you can do this until n=0.
hints.L2: Kernighan's trick: while n > 0: count++; n = n & (n-1). This runs exactly k times where k = number of 1 bits.
hints.L3:
# Number of 1 Bits (Hamming Weight) — O(k) time, O(1) space
# KEY INSIGHT: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit each iteration

def hammingWeight(n):
    count = 0
    while n:
        n &= n - 1  # clear lowest set bit
        count += 1
    return count

# WHY: n-1 flips all bits after lowest set bit, including it
# e.g., n=1100, n-1=1011, n&(n-1)=1000 (cleared lowest set bit)

# ALTERNATIVE: count = bin(n).count('1')  # Pythonic but not O(1)
# ALTERNATIVE: n & 1 then n >>= 1 (always 32 iterations)

follow_up_questions: Why does n & (n-1) always clear exactly one bit?
What's the O(32) naive approach?
How is this used in detecting power of 2 (n & n-1 == 0)?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Kernighan's: n &= n-1 clears lowest set bit. Count iterations until n=0. O(k) where k=set bits.

algo_problems.title: Counting Bits
thinking_questions: What's the relationship between popcount(i) and popcount(i >> 1)?
When you right-shift i by 1, you drop the last bit. How does that affect the count?
Can you use dp where dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1)?

hints.L1: dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1). Right-shifting removes one bit, and you add back whether that bit was 1.
hints.L2: dp[0]=0. For i in 1..n: dp[i] = dp[i//2] + (i % 2). Builds on previous results in O(n) total.
hints.L3:
# Counting Bits — O(n) time, O(n) space
# KEY INSIGHT: dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1)
# i >> 1 drops LSB; add 1 if LSB was set

def countBits(n):
    dp = [0] * (n + 1)

    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1)
        # dp[i//2]: bits in i without last bit
        # (i & 1): 1 if i is odd, 0 if even

    return dp
# Example: dp[7]=dp[3]+1=dp[1]+1+1=dp[0]+1+1+1=3 ✓

follow_up_questions: What other recurrences work? (e.g., dp[i] = dp[i & (i-1)] + 1)
How would you solve this without DP (using Number of 1 Bits for each)?
What's the pattern in the dp array?
algo_problems.solution_notes: dp[i] = dp[i>>1] + (i&1). Right-shift removes LSB, add 1 if it was set. O(n) total.

algo_problems.title: Reverse Bits
thinking_questions: Extract each bit from n (right to left) and place it into result from MSB side.
How do you extract the least significant bit? How do you place a bit at a specific position?
How many iterations do you need regardless of n's value?

hints.L1: For each of 32 bits: extract LSB from n, shift it to the correct position in result, then right-shift n.
hints.L2: result = 0. For i in 0..31: result = (result << 1) | (n & 1); n >>= 1. Result builds from MSB to LSB.
hints.L3:
# Reverse Bits — O(1) time (32 iterations), O(1) space

def reverseBits(n):
    result = 0

    for _ in range(32):
        result = (result << 1) | (n & 1)  # shift result left, add LSB of n
        n >>= 1                            # remove processed bit from n

    return result
# TRACE: n=...1011 → result builds: 1,11,110,1101,...
# After 32 iterations, result has bits of n in reverse order

# PYTHON NOTE: Python ints are arbitrary precision
# Use result & 0xFFFFFFFF if you need unsigned 32-bit

follow_up_questions: If called many times, how would you optimize? (byte-level lookup table)
Why exactly 32 iterations regardless of n's value?
How do you handle Python's arbitrary precision integers?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Build result left-to-right: (result<<1)|(n&1), then n>>=1. Always 32 iterations. O(1).

algo_problems.title: Missing Number
thinking_questions: If you XOR all indices 0..n with all values in nums, what remains?
Alternatively, what's the expected sum vs actual sum?
Can you solve this with O(1) space using either XOR or Gauss's formula?

hints.L1: Expected sum - actual sum = missing number. Or XOR all indices with all values — pairs cancel, missing remains.
hints.L2: Math: return n*(n+1)//2 - sum(nums). XOR: result=n, then XOR with each i and nums[i]. Both O(n) time, O(1) space.

follow_up_questions: Which approach handles overflow better: math or XOR?
What if the array had duplicates instead of one missing?
What if two numbers were missing?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Math: n*(n+1)//2 - sum(nums). XOR: XOR all [0..n] with all nums. Both O(n) time, O(1) space.

algo_problems.title: Sum of Two Integers
thinking_questions: XOR gives sum without carry. AND gives carry positions. How do you combine these?
How do you propagate the carry? What do you shift the carry by?
Python integers are unbounded — how do you handle negative numbers in 32-bit?

hints.L1: a XOR b = sum without carry. (a AND b) << 1 = carry. Repeat until carry is 0.
hints.L2: while b != 0: carry = (a & b) << 1; a = a ^ b; b = carry. Python: mask with 0xFFFFFFFF for 32-bit.

follow_up_questions: Why does XOR give sum without carry?
Why must you AND then shift left by 1 for carry?
How do you handle Python's infinite precision for negative numbers?
algo_problems.solution_notes: XOR=sum, AND<<1=carry. Iterate until carry=0. Python: mask=0xFFFFFFFF for 32-bit correctness.

algo_problems.title: Reverse Integer
thinking_questions: Extract digits one by one using % 10 and build the reversed number.
How do you check for 32-bit integer overflow BEFORE it happens?
Python handles big integers natively — but the problem requires 32-bit overflow behavior.

hints.L1: Extract digits with % 10 and build reverse. Check overflow before multiplying: if result > (INT_MAX - digit) / 10.
hints.L2: while x != 0: digit = x % 10; x //= 10; check if result > (2^31-1-digit)/10; result = result*10 + digit. Return 0 on overflow.
hints.L3:
# Reverse Integer — O(log n) time, O(1) space
# KEY INSIGHT: overflow check BEFORE multiplying by 10

def reverse(x):
    INT_MAX = 2**31 - 1  # 2147483647
    INT_MIN = -2**31     # -2147483648

    result = 0
    sign = 1 if x >= 0 else -1
    x = abs(x)

    while x != 0:
        digit = x % 10
        x //= 10

        # Check overflow before: result*10 + digit > INT_MAX?
        if result > (INT_MAX - digit) // 10:
            return 0

        result = result * 10 + digit

    return sign * result
# NOTE: Python % 10 is always non-negative, so handle sign separately

follow_up_questions: Why check before multiplying instead of after?
How does Python's modulo behavior differ from Java/C for negative numbers?
What if you needed to handle arbitrary precision integers?
algo_problems.solution_notes: Extract digits with %10. Overflow check: result > (INT_MAX - digit) // 10 → return 0. Handle sign separately. O(log n).

questions: What's the key interface that both leaf and composite nodes must implement?
Design a file system using Composite (files and directories).
How does Composite simplify client code when working with tree structures?
What's the tradeoff of Composite (when does it overly generalize)?
How would you implement a total size calculation for a composite file system?
How does Composite relate to recursive data structures?
When would you use Composite vs. a simple recursive function?

patterns.name: Decorator
patterns.description: Attaches additional responsibilities to objects dynamically by wrapping them.
questions: How does Decorator differ from inheritance for adding behavior?
Design a coffee ordering system using Decorator (add milk, sugar, espresso shots).
What's the key requirement for the Decorator to be transparent to clients?
How do Python's @decorators relate to the Decorator design pattern?
What's the order of decoration and why does it matter?

What are the downsides of deep decorator chains?

patterns.name: Facade
patterns.description: Provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem.
questions: What's the difference between Facade and Adapter?
Design a home theater Facade for a complex system of TV, sound, streaming, lights.
Does Facade restrict access to the subsystem, or just provide a simpler interface?
How does Facade help with the 'Law of Demeter'?
When would layered Facades make sense?
How does Facade relate to the 'separation of concerns' principle?
What's the risk of a Facade becoming a 'god object'?

patterns.name: Flyweight
patterns.description: Shares common state among many objects to support large numbers efficiently.
questions: What's the difference between intrinsic and extrinsic state in Flyweight?
Design a text editor using Flyweight for character objects.
When does Flyweight make sense vs. just using a cache?
How does Flyweight factory manage shared instances?
What are the memory vs. CPU tradeoffs in Flyweight?
How would you implement Flyweight in a particle system for a game?
What makes Flyweight objects immutable by necessity?

patterns.name: Proxy
patterns.description: Provides a surrogate or placeholder to control access to another object.
questions: What are the 4 main types of Proxy (virtual, protection, remote, logging)?
Design a caching proxy for an expensive database query object.
How does Proxy differ from Decorator?
How would you implement a lazy-loading proxy for a large image?
When would you use a protection proxy for access control?
How does a remote proxy hide network communication from clients?
What's the interface requirement for Proxy to be transparent?

patterns.name: Chain of Responsibility
patterns.description: Passes requests along a chain of handlers, each deciding to handle or pass on.
questions: How does Chain of Responsibility decouple senders from receivers?
Design a support ticket escalation system (L1→L2→L3) using CoR.
What's the difference between Chain of Responsibility and a pipeline?
How do you handle the case where no handler handles the request?
When would you choose CoR over a simple if-else chain?
How does CoR relate to middleware in web frameworks like Express.js?
What are the performance implications of long chains?

patterns.name: Command
patterns.description: Encapsulates a request as an object, enabling undo/redo, queuing, and logging.
questions: What are the 4 components of Command pattern (invoker, command, receiver, client)?
Design an undo/redo system for a text editor using Command.
How does Command enable transaction-like behavior?
How does Command support queuing and scheduled execution?
When would you use Command vs. a callback function?
How does Command implement the 'encapsulate what varies' principle?
Design a macro-recording feature using Command.

patterns.name: Iterator
patterns.description: Provides a way to access elements of a collection sequentially without exposing its implementation.
questions: What interface must an Iterator implement?
How does Iterator decouple traversal logic from the collection?
Design a tree iterator that traverses nodes in-order without exposing tree internals.
How do Python's __iter__ and __next__ implement the Iterator pattern?
What's the difference between internal and external iterators?
When would you implement a custom iterator vs. use a generator?
How do lazy iterators save memory vs. materializing a full list?

patterns.name: Mediator
patterns.description: Reduces direct dependencies between components by centralizing communication.
questions: How does Mediator reduce the number of connections between components?
Design a chat room using Mediator where users communicate through a central server.
What's the risk of Mediator becoming a 'god object'?
How does Mediator differ from Observer?
When should you use Mediator vs. direct component communication?
How does the air traffic control system exemplify Mediator?
How does Mediator relate to the 'star topology' vs. 'mesh topology' tradeoff?

patterns.name: Memento
patterns.description: Captures and externalizes an object's internal state so it can be restored later.
questions: What are the 3 participants of Memento (originator, memento, caretaker)?
How does Memento preserve encapsulation while saving state?
Design an undo/redo system for a drawing app using Memento.
How does Memento differ from Command pattern for implementing undo?
What are the memory implications of storing many mementos?
How would you implement incremental mementos instead of full snapshots?
When is Memento better than serialization for state capture?

patterns.name: Observer
patterns.description: Defines a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state, dependents are notified.
questions: What's the difference between push and pull notification in Observer?
Design an event system for a stock ticker using Observer.
What's the 'dangling observer' problem and how do you avoid it?
How does Observer relate to the pub/sub pattern?
How does Observer implement the dependency inversion principle?
When would you use Observer vs. direct method calls?
How do you handle ordering guarantees with multiple observers?

patterns.name: State
patterns.description: Allows an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes.
questions: How does State eliminate complex if/else or switch statements?
Design a traffic light system using State pattern.
What's the difference between State and Strategy pattern?
When does a state machine become complex enough to warrant the State pattern?
How do state objects transition to other states?
Can a State have multiple contexts? What are the implications?
Design a vending machine with State (idle, has_money, dispensing, out_of_stock).

patterns.name: Strategy
patterns.description: Defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one, and makes them interchangeable.
questions: How does Strategy implement the Open/Closed Principle?
Design a sorting strategy system where the algorithm is selected at runtime.
What's the difference between Strategy and State?
How does Strategy eliminate conditional logic in the context class?
When would you use Strategy vs. a simple function parameter?

patterns.name: Template Method
patterns.description: Defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base class, deferring some steps to subclasses.
questions: What's the difference between Template Method and Strategy?

What are 'hooks' in Template Method and when do you use them?

How does Template Method encourage code reuse across subclasses?

patterns.name: Visitor
patterns.description: Lets you add new operations to objects without modifying their classes.
questions: What's 'double dispatch' and how does Visitor use it?

aiml.description: AI/ML curriculum: math refresher → core ML → deep learning → frontier → ML system design

math_refresher.title: Vectors and Vector Operations
math_refresher.summary: Vectors as geometric objects and data containers. Operations that underpin all of ML.
key_concepts: Vector addition and scalar multiplication
Dot product: a·b = |a||b|cos(θ) — measures similarity
L1 and L2 norms — regularization foundations
Orthogonality: a·b = 0 means independent features
Unit vectors and normalization
math_refresher.ml_connection: Every data point is a vector. Dot products appear in linear regression, SVMs, attention mechanisms, and cosine similarity.
exercises: Given vectors a=[1,2,3] and b=[4,5,6], compute a·b, ||a||_2, and the angle between them.
Why does L2 regularization penalize large weights? Show mathematically using the norm.
In Python with numpy: implement cosine similarity between two word embedding vectors.
Why is orthogonality important for features in a dataset? Give an example of highly collinear features causing problems.
math_refresher.code_example:
import numpy as np

a = np.array([1, 2, 3])
b = np.array([4, 5, 6])

dot = np.dot(a, b)                          # 32
l2_norm = np.linalg.norm(a)                 # 3.742
cosine_sim = dot / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))  # 0.974
angle = np.arccos(cosine_sim) * 180 / np.pi  # degrees
thinking_questions: What does a high cosine similarity between two vectors mean geometrically?
Why is L2 norm differentiable everywhere but L1 is not at 0?

math_refresher.title: Matrices and Linear Transformations
math_refresher.summary: Matrices as functions that transform vectors. Foundation for neural network layers.
key_concepts: Matrix multiplication as function composition
Matrix as a linear transformation (rotation, scaling, projection)
Transpose: (AB)^T = B^T A^T
Identity matrix, inverse matrix
Rank and null space
math_refresher.ml_connection: Every neural network layer is a matrix multiplication followed by a nonlinearity. Weight matrix W transforms input x to output Wx.
exercises: What does a 2x2 rotation matrix look like? Apply it to vector [1,0] and [0,1].
Why can't you invert a non-square matrix? What does rank tell you?
In numpy: implement a forward pass of one linear layer (no bias) for a batch of 32 samples with 10 features each, mapping to 5 outputs.
Show that matrix multiplication is NOT commutative (AB ≠ BA) with a simple example.
math_refresher.code_example:
import numpy as np

# Linear layer: batch of 32 samples, 10 features → 5 outputs
X = np.random.randn(32, 10)  # input batch
W = np.random.randn(10, 5)   # weight matrix
b = np.random.randn(5)        # bias

output = X @ W + b            # shape: (32, 5)
thinking_questions: What transformation does a diagonal matrix perform?
If W has rank < n, what information is lost in the transformation Wx?

math_refresher.title: Eigenvalues, Eigenvectors, and Decomposition
math_refresher.summary: Eigenvectors reveal the 'natural axes' of a transformation. Foundation for PCA, SVD, and spectral methods.
key_concepts: Av = λv — eigenvector v is only scaled, not rotated
Characteristic equation: det(A - λI) = 0
Symmetric matrices have orthogonal eigenvectors
Eigendecomposition: A = QΛQ^T (for symmetric A)
Spectral theorem: real symmetric matrices are always diagonalizable
math_refresher.ml_connection: PCA finds eigenvectors of the covariance matrix. PageRank uses the dominant eigenvector. Kernel methods use eigendecomposition.
exercises: Find eigenvalues and eigenvectors of [[2,1],[1,2]] by hand.
What do large vs small eigenvalues of the covariance matrix tell you about the data?
In numpy: compute eigendecomposition of a covariance matrix from a 100x5 dataset.
Why must the covariance matrix always have non-negative eigenvalues?
math_refresher.code_example:
import numpy as np

# Covariance matrix eigendecomposition
data = np.random.randn(100, 5)
cov = np.cov(data.T)                          # 5x5 covariance matrix
eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eigh(cov)  # sorted ascending

# Variance explained by each component
explained_variance = eigenvalues / eigenvalues.sum()
thinking_questions: What does it mean geometrically when a matrix has a zero eigenvalue?
Why are eigenvectors of a symmetric matrix orthogonal to each other?

math_refresher.title: SVD and PCA
math_refresher.summary: SVD is the most important matrix decomposition in ML. PCA is its direct application for dimensionality reduction.
key_concepts: A = UΣV^T — any matrix, not just square
U: left singular vectors (output space), V: right singular vectors (input space), Σ: singular values
Low-rank approximation: keep top-k singular values
PCA = SVD on centered data
Explained variance ratio from singular values
math_refresher.ml_connection: Used in: PCA, LSA (NLP), collaborative filtering (matrix factorization), image compression, word embeddings (GloVe).
exercises: Implement PCA from scratch using SVD on the iris dataset. Project to 2D and plot.
What is the relationship between SVD and eigendecomposition?
Why do we center data before PCA?
How would you choose the number of components k? What threshold for explained variance?
math_refresher.code_example:
import numpy as np

# PCA from scratch using SVD
data = np.random.randn(100, 10)
data_centered = data - data.mean(axis=0)

U, S, Vt = np.linalg.svd(data_centered, full_matrices=False)

# Project to 2D (keep top 2 components)
k = 2
data_2d = data_centered @ Vt[:k].T  # shape: (100, 2)

# Explained variance
explained = (S**2) / (S**2).sum()
print(f'Top 2 components explain {explained[:k].sum():.1%} of variance')
thinking_questions: Why is SVD more numerically stable than directly computing eigenvalues of A^T A?
How is PCA related to finding the axes of maximum variance?

math_refresher.title: Matrix Calculus and Gradients
math_refresher.summary: Extending calculus to vectors and matrices. This is the backbone of backpropagation.
key_concepts: Gradient ∇f: vector of partial derivatives
Jacobian: matrix of partial derivatives for vector-valued functions
Hessian: matrix of second derivatives (curvature)
Chain rule for vectors: dL/dx = (dL/dy)(dy/dx)
Common gradients: d/dx(Wx) = W^T, d/dx(x^T Ax) = 2Ax
math_refresher.ml_connection: Backpropagation is repeated application of the chain rule on matrix operations. Every gradient update in neural networks is matrix calculus.
exercises: Derive the gradient of MSE loss L = ||Wx - y||^2 with respect to W.
What is the Jacobian of the softmax function?
In numpy: verify your analytical gradient for L=||Wx-y||^2 using numerical differentiation.
Why does the Hessian being positive definite indicate a minimum?
math_refresher.code_example:
import numpy as np

# Gradient verification using finite differences
def mse_loss(W, X, y):
    pred = X @ W
    return np.mean((pred - y)**2)

def analytical_gradient(W, X, y):
    pred = X @ W
    return 2 * X.T @ (pred - y) / len(y)

# Numerical gradient check
W = np.random.randn(5, 1)
X = np.random.randn(10, 5)
y = np.random.randn(10, 1)
eps = 1e-5

numerical_grad = np.zeros_like(W)
for i in range(W.shape[0]):
    W_plus = W.copy(); W_plus[i] += eps
    W_minus = W.copy(); W_minus[i] -= eps
    numerical_grad[i] = (mse_loss(W_plus, X, y) - mse_loss(W_minus, X, y)) / (2*eps)

analytical = analytical_gradient(W, X, y)
print('Max diff:', np.max(np.abs(numerical_grad - analytical)))  # should be ~1e-7
thinking_questions: Why do we transpose weight matrices when computing gradients?
What does a zero gradient tell you about a point? Can it be a saddle point?

math_refresher.title: Gradient Descent and Optimization
math_refresher.summary: How ML models learn: iteratively moving in the direction of steepest descent.
key_concepts: Gradient descent: θ ← θ - η∇L(θ)
Learning rate η: too large (diverge), too small (slow)
SGD vs Mini-batch vs Full-batch
Momentum, Adam, RMSProp
Convex vs non-convex loss landscapes
Local minima, saddle points, vanishing gradients
math_refresher.ml_connection: Every ML model trains by gradient descent. Understanding optimizers is critical for debugging training instability.
exercises: Implement vanilla gradient descent to minimize f(x) = x^2 + 2x + 1.
Implement SGD with momentum from scratch on a simple linear regression.
Why does Adam work better than SGD in practice for deep learning?
What is the 'learning rate warmup' trick and why does it help?
thinking_questions: Why does momentum help escape shallow local minima?
What's the difference between a saddle point and a local minimum?

math_refresher.title: Probability Distributions
math_refresher.summary: Core distributions that appear constantly in ML: Gaussian, Bernoulli, Categorical, Beta, Dirichlet.
key_concepts: Gaussian N(μ,σ²): bell curve, sum of many independent variables (CLT)
Bernoulli/Binomial: binary outcomes
Categorical/Multinomial: multi-class classification
Poisson: count data
Exponential family: unified framework for many distributions
KL Divergence: D_KL(P||Q) = E[log P/Q] — measures distribution difference
math_refresher.ml_connection: Loss functions arise from distribution assumptions. MSE assumes Gaussian noise. Cross-entropy assumes Categorical/Bernoulli. VAEs use KL divergence.
exercises: Derive MSE loss from the assumption that noise is Gaussian (MLE derivation).
Derive cross-entropy loss from the categorical distribution MLE.
Compute KL divergence between two Gaussians analytically.
In numpy: sample from a Gaussian mixture model with 3 components.
math_refresher.code_example:
import numpy as np

# KL divergence between two Gaussians (analytical formula)
def kl_gaussian(mu1, sigma1, mu2, sigma2):
    return (np.log(sigma2/sigma1) +
            (sigma1**2 + (mu1-mu2)**2) / (2*sigma2**2) - 0.5)

# Sample from Gaussian Mixture Model
def sample_gmm(means, stds, weights, n=1000):
    components = np.random.choice(len(means), size=n, p=weights)
    samples = np.array([np.random.normal(means[c], stds[c]) for c in components])
    return samples
thinking_questions: Why is KL divergence not symmetric? When does that matter?
What distribution assumption underlies L1 loss (MAE)?

math_refresher.title: Bayesian Thinking: MLE, MAP, and Bayes
math_refresher.summary: The probabilistic framework underlying all of ML. MLE, MAP, and full Bayesian inference.
key_concepts: MLE: find θ that maximizes P(data|θ)
MAP: find θ that maximizes P(θ|data) ∝ P(data|θ)P(θ)
Bayes: P(θ|data) = P(data|θ)P(θ) / P(data)
Prior, likelihood, posterior, evidence
L2 regularization = Gaussian prior (MAP)
L1 regularization = Laplace prior (MAP)
math_refresher.ml_connection: Every regularized ML model is doing MAP estimation. Dropout is approximate Bayesian inference. Uncertainty quantification requires Bayesian thinking.
exercises: Show that ridge regression (L2) is equivalent to MAP with Gaussian prior.
Show that lasso regression (L1) is equivalent to MAP with Laplace prior.
Implement Naive Bayes classifier from scratch for text classification.
What is the 'evidence' P(data) in Bayes theorem and why is it hard to compute?
thinking_questions: When would you prefer MAP over MLE?
What happens to the posterior as you collect more data?

math_refresher.title: Information Theory for ML
math_refresher.summary: Entropy, mutual information, and KL divergence — the theoretical foundation of loss functions and feature selection.
key_concepts: Entropy H(X) = -Σ p(x) log p(x) — measures uncertainty
Cross-entropy H(P,Q) = -Σ p(x) log q(x) — used as loss function
KL Divergence = Cross-entropy - Entropy
Mutual Information I(X;Y) — feature relevance measure
Information gain — used in decision trees
math_refresher.ml_connection: Cross-entropy loss for classification. Information gain for tree splits. KL divergence in VAEs and distillation. Mutual information in representation learning.
exercises: Compute entropy of a fair coin vs a biased coin (p=0.9).
Show that cross-entropy loss = negative log likelihood for categorical distribution.
Implement information gain from scratch and use it to pick the best split for a decision tree.
What is the relationship between cross-entropy and KL divergence?
thinking_questions: Why is entropy maximized for a uniform distribution?
Why can KL(P||Q) be infinite even when KL(Q||P) is finite?

math_refresher.title: Statistical Learning Theory
math_refresher.summary: Bias-variance tradeoff, generalization bounds, and the foundations of why ML works.
key_concepts: Bias-variance decomposition: Error = Bias² + Variance + Noise
Overfitting vs underfitting
Empirical risk minimization
VC dimension and sample complexity
Cross-validation: k-fold, stratified, leave-one-out
Regularization as complexity control
math_refresher.ml_connection: Every model selection decision involves the bias-variance tradeoff. Cross-validation is standard practice. Regularization is the primary tool against overfitting.
exercises: Draw the bias-variance tradeoff curve as model complexity increases.
Implement k-fold cross-validation from scratch.
Show empirically that increasing polynomial degree eventually overfits.
What is the double descent phenomenon and why does it challenge classical ML theory?
math_refresher.code_example:
import numpy as np

def k_fold_cv(model_class, X, y, k=5):
    n = len(X)
    indices = np.arange(n)
    np.random.shuffle(indices)
    folds = np.array_split(indices, k)
    scores = []

    for i in range(k):
        val_idx = folds[i]
        train_idx = np.concatenate([folds[j] for j in range(k) if j != i])

        model = model_class()
        model.fit(X[train_idx], y[train_idx])
        score = model.score(X[val_idx], y[val_idx])
        scores.append(score)

    return np.mean(scores), np.std(scores)
thinking_questions: Why does adding more training data reduce variance but not bias?
When would you choose a high-bias, low-variance model over the reverse?

core_ml.title: Linear Regression from Scratch
core_ml.summary: The simplest ML model. Master this and you understand gradient descent, loss functions, and overfitting.
key_concepts: Hypothesis: ŷ = Xθ
MSE loss: L = (1/n)||Xθ - y||²
Normal equation: θ = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y
Gradient descent solution
Ridge regression (L2): θ = (X^T X + λI)^{-1} X^T y
Lasso regression (L1): no closed form, use coordinate descent
core_ml.ml_connection: Foundation for understanding all supervised learning. The math here extends directly to logistic regression and neural networks.
exercises: Implement linear regression using the normal equation. Compare to gradient descent solution.
Implement ridge regression. Show how λ affects the coefficient magnitudes.
Implement lasso with coordinate descent. Show sparsity of coefficients.
Implement polynomial regression and show overfitting at high degree.
core_ml.code_example:
import numpy as np

class LinearRegression:
    def fit_normal_eq(self, X, y):
        X_b = np.c_[np.ones((len(X), 1)), X]  # add bias column
        self.theta = np.linalg.pinv(X_b.T @ X_b) @ X_b.T @ y
        return self

    def fit_gd(self, X, y, lr=0.01, epochs=1000):
        X_b = np.c_[np.ones((len(X), 1)), X]
        self.theta = np.zeros(X_b.shape[1])
        n = len(y)
        for _ in range(epochs):
            grad = (2/n) * X_b.T @ (X_b @ self.theta - y)
            self.theta -= lr * grad
        return self

    def predict(self, X):
        X_b = np.c_[np.ones((len(X), 1)), X]
        return X_b @ self.theta

    def r2_score(self, X, y):
        y_pred = self.predict(X)
        ss_res = np.sum((y - y_pred)**2)
        ss_tot = np.sum((y - y.mean())**2)
        return 1 - ss_res / ss_tot
thinking_questions: When does the normal equation fail? (Hint: what if X^T X is singular?)
Why does ridge regression always have a unique solution even when X^T X is singular?

core_ml.title: Logistic Regression from Scratch
core_ml.summary: Binary classification using sigmoid activation and cross-entropy loss.
key_concepts: Sigmoid: σ(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})
Binary cross-entropy: L = -[y log(ŷ) + (1-y) log(1-ŷ)]
No closed form — must use gradient descent
Decision boundary is linear in feature space
Multiclass via softmax + categorical cross-entropy
Regularization: C parameter in sklearn
core_ml.ml_connection: Last layer of any binary classifier. Understanding sigmoid + cross-entropy is essential for neural network output layers.
exercises: Implement logistic regression with gradient descent from scratch.
Implement softmax regression (multinomial logistic) for 3-class classification.
Show that the gradient of cross-entropy + sigmoid simplifies beautifully to ŷ - y.
Implement L2-regularized logistic regression.
thinking_questions: Why can't you use MSE loss with logistic regression? (Hint: non-convex)
What is the log-odds interpretation of logistic regression coefficients?

core_ml.title: Decision Trees from Scratch
core_ml.summary: Tree-based models using information gain / Gini impurity for splitting.
key_concepts: Gini impurity: 1 - Σ p_i²
Information gain: H(parent) - weighted_H(children)
Greedy splitting: try all features and thresholds
Max depth, min samples as regularization

core_ml.title: K-Means Clustering from Scratch
core_ml.summary: The most fundamental clustering algorithm. EM-style optimization via alternating assignment and update.
key_concepts: Algorithm: assign points to nearest centroid, update centroids, repeat
Convergence guaranteed but to local minimum
K-means++ initialization for better results
Elbow method and silhouette score for choosing k
Limitations: assumes spherical clusters, sensitive to outliers
Time complexity: O(nkdi) — n points, k clusters, d dims, i iterations
core_ml.ml_connection: Used for customer segmentation, document clustering, image compression, initialization for other algorithms.

core_ml.code_example:
import numpy as np

class KMeans:
    def __init__(self, k, max_iter=100, tol=1e-4):
        self.k = k; self.max_iter = max_iter; self.tol = tol

    def fit(self, X):
        # K-means++ initialization
        centroids = [X[np.random.randint(len(X))]]
        for _ in range(self.k - 1):
            dists = np.min([np.sum((X - c)**2, axis=1) for c in centroids], axis=0)
            probs = dists / dists.sum()
            centroids.append(X[np.random.choice(len(X), p=probs)])
        self.centroids = np.array(centroids)

        for _ in range(self.max_iter):
            # Assign
            dists = np.array([np.sum((X - c)**2, axis=1) for c in self.centroids])
            labels = np.argmin(dists, axis=0)
            # Update
            new_centroids = np.array([X[labels == i].mean(axis=0) for i in range(self.k)])
            if np.max(np.abs(new_centroids - self.centroids)) < self.tol:
                break
            self.centroids = new_centroids
        self.labels_ = labels
        return self

core_ml.code_example:
import numpy as np

def precision_recall_f1(y_true, y_pred):
    tp = np.sum((y_pred == 1) & (y_true == 1))
    fp = np.sum((y_pred == 1) & (y_true == 0))
    fn = np.sum((y_pred == 0) & (y_true == 1))
    precision = tp / (tp + fp + 1e-9)
    recall = tp / (tp + fn + 1e-9)
    f1 = 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall + 1e-9)
    return precision, recall, f1

def roc_auc(y_true, y_scores):
    thresholds = np.sort(np.unique(y_scores))[::-1]
    tprs, fprs = [], []
    for t in thresholds:
        y_pred = (y_scores >= t).astype(int)
        tp = np.sum((y_pred == 1) & (y_true == 1))
        fp = np.sum((y_pred == 1) & (y_true == 0))
        fn = np.sum((y_pred == 0) & (y_true == 1))
        tn = np.sum((y_pred == 0) & (y_true == 0))
        tprs.append(tp / (tp + fn + 1e-9))
        fprs.append(fp / (fp + tn + 1e-9))
    auc = np.trapz(tprs, fprs)
    return abs(auc)

deep_learning.title: Neural Networks and Backpropagation from Scratch
deep_learning.summary: Build a 2-layer neural network from scratch. Understand forward pass, loss, and backprop fully.
key_concepts: Forward pass: z = Wx + b, a = activation(z)
Activation functions: ReLU, sigmoid, tanh, GELU

dL/dW = dL/da * da/dz * dz/dW
Vanishing/exploding gradients
Weight initialization: Xavier, He

deep_learning.code_example:
import numpy as np

class MLP:
    def __init__(self, input_dim, hidden_dim, output_dim, lr=0.01):
        # He initialization for ReLU
        self.W1 = np.random.randn(input_dim, hidden_dim) * np.sqrt(2/input_dim)
        self.b1 = np.zeros(hidden_dim)
        self.W2 = np.random.randn(hidden_dim, output_dim) * np.sqrt(2/hidden_dim)
        self.b2 = np.zeros(output_dim)
        self.lr = lr

    def relu(self, z): return np.maximum(0, z)
    def sigmoid(self, z): return 1/(1+np.exp(-z))

    def forward(self, X):
        self.z1 = X @ self.W1 + self.b1
        self.a1 = self.relu(self.z1)
        self.z2 = self.a1 @ self.W2 + self.b2
        self.a2 = self.sigmoid(self.z2)
        return self.a2

    def backward(self, X, y):
        n = len(y)
        dL_da2 = self.a2 - y.reshape(-1,1)          # BCE gradient
        dL_dW2 = self.a1.T @ dL_da2 / n
        dL_db2 = dL_da2.mean(axis=0)
        dL_da1 = dL_da2 @ self.W2.T
        dL_dz1 = dL_da1 * (self.z1 > 0)            # ReLU gradient
        dL_dW1 = X.T @ dL_dz1 / n
        dL_db1 = dL_dz1.mean(axis=0)
        # Update
        self.W2 -= self.lr * dL_dW2
        self.b2 -= self.lr * dL_db2
        self.W1 -= self.lr * dL_dW1
        self.b1 -= self.lr * dL_db1

deep_learning.code_example:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class ResidualBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, channels):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1)
        self.bn1 = nn.BatchNorm2d(channels)
        self.conv2 = nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1)
        self.bn2 = nn.BatchNorm2d(channels)
        self.relu = nn.ReLU()

    def forward(self, x):
        residual = x
        out = self.relu(self.bn1(self.conv1(x)))
        out = self.bn2(self.conv2(out))
        return self.relu(out + residual)  # skip connection

class SimpleCNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, num_classes=10):
        super().__init__()
        self.features = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(1, 32, 3, padding=1), nn.ReLU(), nn.MaxPool2d(2),
            ResidualBlock(32),
            nn.Conv2d(32, 64, 3, padding=1), nn.ReLU(), nn.MaxPool2d(2)
        )
        self.classifier = nn.Linear(64*7*7, num_classes)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.features(x)
        return self.classifier(x.flatten(1))

deep_learning.title: Attention Mechanism and Transformers
deep_learning.summary: The architecture behind GPT, BERT, and all modern LLMs. Understand this deeply.
key_concepts: Self-attention: Q, K, V = XW_Q, XW_K, XW_V
Attention scores: softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k))V
Multi-head attention: parallel attention heads concatenated
Positional encoding: inject position information
Transformer block: attention + FFN + LayerNorm + residual
Encoder vs Decoder vs Encoder-Decoder architectures
exercises: Implement scaled dot-product attention from scratch in numpy.

What is the time and space complexity of self-attention? (O(n^2 d)) — why is this a problem for long sequences?
deep_learning.code_example:
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Scaled dot-product attention (numpy)
def attention(Q, K, V, mask=None):
    d_k = Q.shape[-1]
    scores = Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1) / np.sqrt(d_k)
    if mask is not None:
        scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, -1e9)
    weights = np.softmax(scores, axis=-1)
    return weights @ V, weights

# Multi-head attention (PyTorch)
class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, n_heads):
        super().__init__()
        self.d_k = d_model // n_heads
        self.n_heads = n_heads
        self.W_Q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.W_K = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.W_V = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.W_O = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)

    def forward(self, x, mask=None):
        B, T, D = x.shape
        Q = self.W_Q(x).view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1,2)
        K = self.W_K(x).view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1,2)
        V = self.W_V(x).view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1,2)
        scores = (Q @ K.transpose(-2,-1)) / self.d_k**0.5
        weights = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
        out = (weights @ V).transpose(1,2).contiguous().view(B, T, D)
        return self.W_O(out)

deep_learning.title: Recurrent Networks and Sequence Modeling

key_concepts: RNN: h_t = tanh(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t)

deep_learning.code_example:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class LSTMCell(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size):
        super().__init__()
        self.hidden_size = hidden_size
        # Combined gate weights
        self.W = nn.Linear(input_size + hidden_size, 4 * hidden_size)

    def forward(self, x, state):
        h, c = state
        combined = torch.cat([x, h], dim=1)
        gates = self.W(combined)
        f, i, g, o = gates.chunk(4, dim=1)  # forget, input, cell, output

        f = torch.sigmoid(f)
        i = torch.sigmoid(i)
        g = torch.tanh(g)
        o = torch.sigmoid(o)

        c_new = f * c + i * g     # forget old + write new
        h_new = o * torch.tanh(c_new)  # output gated cell state
        return h_new, c_new

frontier.code_example:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class GPTBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, n_heads, d_ff, dropout=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, n_heads, dropout=dropout, batch_first=True)
        self.ff = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff), nn.GELU(), nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model))
        self.ln1 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.ln2 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.drop = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, x, causal_mask=None):
        # Pre-norm architecture (GPT-2 style)
        attn_out, _ = self.attn(self.ln1(x), self.ln1(x), self.ln1(x), attn_mask=causal_mask)
        x = x + self.drop(attn_out)
        x = x + self.drop(self.ff(self.ln2(x)))
        return x

frontier.title: Diffusion Models
frontier.summary: The generative model behind Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, and Sora. Your PDE background gives you an edge here.

frontier.code_example:
import numpy as np
import torch

# DDPM forward process
def cosine_schedule(T, s=0.008):
    t = np.arange(T+1)
    f = np.cos((t/T + s)/(1+s) * np.pi/2)**2
    alphas = np.clip(f[1:]/f[:-1], 0.0001, 0.9999)
    return torch.tensor(alphas, dtype=torch.float32)

def add_noise(x0, t, alphas_cumprod):
    sqrt_alpha = alphas_cumprod[t].sqrt().view(-1,1,1,1)
    sqrt_one_minus = (1-alphas_cumprod[t]).sqrt().view(-1,1,1,1)
    noise = torch.randn_like(x0)
    noisy = sqrt_alpha * x0 + sqrt_one_minus * noise
    return noisy, noise
